⏱Flight Time Calibration
Enter two reference flights to power en-route time estimates across the app — on route lines, the map, measurements, and airport cards.
Shortest nm min
Longest nm min

* All figures are rough estimates for planning purposes only — use SimBrief for more accurate final calculations. Adj load figures are weight-corrected at sea level; per-airport density-altitude correction is applied in the matrix tooltips and dimming, not here.

Range scaling model

Adj range = base × ln(TOW / ZFW) ÷ ln(MTOW / ZFWpub)

ZFW = OEW + payload; ZFWpub = MTOW − max fuel. Breguet range equation, per-airframe OEW & fuel data.

Performance suppressed when payload + fuel exceeds MTOW. TO & LDG use weight² for all types.

✈  SimBrief Flight Plan Settings
Saved locally in your browser. Never sent to SimFly. Generate or look up at simbrief.com → Account → Pilot ID.
When on, each waypoint ident is shown as a permanent label next to its dot. When off, idents only appear on hover.
Applies to the route polyline, waypoint dots, and origin / destination rings.
🎲  Random Flight Filters

Generate a random route to another pilot’s SimFly-owned airport. Every filter below stacks — leave a field blank, a list empty, or the toggle off for no limit. Saved in this browser.

Respects the active map filters (category, level, owner, country, region, continent, developer, excludes, pins).

🗂  Map Layers

Set what draws on top of what. Drag a layer up — or use the ▲▼ buttons — to lift it above the others. The top of the list is drawn on top of everything. Changes apply instantly and are saved in this browser.

🎬  Flight Demo Mode
Drive AA's telemetry and alerting system with a synthetic flight along a saved plan — no MSFS or FSUIPC connection required. Perfect for alert testing or offline demos.
Flight Plan
No flight plan loaded
Save takes the currently-loaded SimBrief flight plan and writes a JSON file with the departure, destination, and every waypoint (name + lat/lon). Load reads that file back so you can demo at sites that block SimBrief.
Aircraft
Sorted by SimFly category (1 → 7), then alphabetically. The demo synthesizes climb / cruise / descent performance per category — Cat 1 (Light GA) tops out at ~4,000 ft / 110 kt, Cat 7 (widebody) cruises at FL380 / 470 kt. Defaults to your current AA aircraft pick, or a Cat 6 aircraft if none is selected.
Start
Starting the demo loads the floating control bar at the bottom of the map. While running, the FSUIPC / STKP / SimBrief Live-menu buttons are disabled to prevent the marker getting yanked out from under the synthetic packets — the rest of the map stays fully interactive.
📍  Aircraft Marker Icon
Pick the silhouette used for the live-aircraft marker on the map. Your choice is saved locally and applies the moment you click — no Save button needed.
📈  Live Flight Data —
Live flight Reference flight Calculated line
DateDistanceTime
?  How to Use This Tool
Some PC-only features (live aircraft tracking, AFK alarms, the data telemetry panel, etc.) are hidden from this help on touch devices because they require a connected flight simulator. They’re documented in full on the desktop version of the app.
⏳
Startup & Loading Splash
On launch (v3.4.0+) a splash screen shows a progress bar and the name of each component as it loads — the airport database, region names, runways, airport categories, the aircraft database, field overrides, pilot payouts, then route-distance computation and the owner airport list. On a fast connection the names flick by too quickly to read; that’s expected. The splash fades on its own once everything is ready, and a built-in safety timeout dismisses it even if a data source is slow or unreachable, so it can never trap you on the loading screen.
🧭
Startup Choice — Owner View, Explore Map, or SimBrief
After the loading splash fades (v3.7.0+) a choice screen asks what you’d like to do. Three options are available on desktop; two on mobile (the SimBrief option requires a PC).

Load SimBrief Flight Plan (desktop only, v3.21.0+) — appears centered above the other two cards. Click it to import your latest SimBrief OFP and open the map with the Alerts panel ready to configure. If your SimBrief Pilot ID isn’t saved yet you’ll be prompted for it once; it’s stored locally for future sessions. The flight plan is fetched and plotted on the map automatically, then the Alerts panel opens so you can set up waypoint, cruise, descent, or timer alarms before you even take off. Cancelling the Pilot ID prompt returns you to the choice screen.

Show airports by Owner takes you to the classic distance matrix — pick this when you already know which pilots own the airports you want for departure and arrival. Explore map opens the interactive map for browsing by location, payout, region, continent, developer, drawing region circles, tracking flights, and setting up in-flight alerts.

A small Skip link (or pressing Esc) dismisses the choice and drops you on the matrix. The choice is bypassed automatically when Recip Tracker or RM Lite hands you off with a specific intent — a dep/arr/depOwner/arrOwner URL parameter sends you straight to the matrix with the route prefilled, and a pilots= URL parameter opens the map pre-filtered to those owners. The choice only appears on a plain startup with no parameters.
🛩
Aircraft Rentals
Turn on Aircraft Rentals from the map menu (v3.50.0+) to see where SimFly’s rentable aircraft are. Every airport’s dot recolors by the best pilot payout available there — the cut the renting pilot keeps, from 0% up to 60% — and a Rentals panel opens for filtering.

Stays current automatically (v3.60.0+): the rental and airport feeds refresh quietly in the background every 20 minutes — and the moment you enter the mode — so a tab left open for hours won’t show stale availability. The refresh happens in place: no page reload, and your map position, selected airports, routes, regions, and filters are all preserved. A small “↻ Rental data synced N min ago” line at the bottom of the panel shows how fresh the data is; click it to refresh on demand.

On the map, each airport can show a small stack of chips listing the aircraft types available there. The badges thin out automatically when you’re zoomed out and reveal more (up to ten chips per airport) as you zoom in; when space is tight, your own SimFly-owned airports get priority. Hover a badge for a full breakdown — type, registration, owner, payout, and live availability for every matching aircraft.

The panel lets you filter by aircraft category (the 1–7 circles, with All / None / Invert), set a minimum payout by dragging the gradient bar (right-click it to recolor), pick aircraft types and owners, toggle “available now only,” and toggle “Only airports already on the map” (v3.62.0) to consider just the SimFly-owned airports already shown (still subject to any active filters) instead of pulling in every rental-only airport from across the world — the map dots, results, counts, and totals all stay limited to your airports. Each section collapses, and the panel is draggable and resizable. The results table lists every matching aircraft — sort by any column, group by owner / airport / type, drag the dividers between columns to resize them (double-click to auto-fit), and click any row to fly to that airport. Aircraft blocked by a ground-operations timer show their countdown in red.

Closing the panel with its ✕ leaves the rental colors and badges on the map; use the menu item or the breadcrumb pill (click it to show/hide the panel, ✕ to exit) to turn the whole view off.
📋
Aircraft Listing
Aircraft Listing (in the 🛩 Rentals menu, v3.66.0+) opens a large, resizable, draggable panel with a full table of every rentable aircraft — one row each — showing its airport, airport owner, aircraft ICAO, manufacturer, type, aircraft owner, pilot payout %, and availability (airport owner and aircraft owner are often different — a plane can be parked at another pilot’s airport). For aircraft in ground operations the Available column shows a live hours / minutes / seconds countdown until it frees up, and Avail Time (Local) shows the local clock time it becomes available.

Click any column heading to sort A–Z, then again for Z–A. Each heading’s ▾ opens a filter: a multi-select list for most columns, a minimum payout % for Payout, a minimum airport category for Airport, and a Yes / No choice for Available. Filters combine across columns.

The table drives the map — whenever it’s filtered, the map shows only the airport dots still in the table. Click an airport to pan to it; click an owner, aircraft, manufacturer, or type to narrow to just those (click the same value again to clear it). By default the panel opens just wide enough to fit every column heading, with longer values trimmed (hover a cell to read the full text). Drag a column divider to widen a column, or double-click a divider to reset all columns back to fitting their titles. A slider in the title bar sets the panel’s opacity so you can see the map underneath (the Aircraft Rentals panel has the same control). ↺ Reset clears all filters; your sort, widths, filters, opacity, and panel size are remembered between sessions.
🛩
Find Your Aircraft Across an Owner’s Fleet
Pick an aircraft (👁 Display → Select Aircraft), then select a departure or arrival airport on the map. AA badges every other airport owned by that airport’s owner that has your aircraft rentable right now (no ground-ops timer), each linked back to your selected airport by a faint dotted green line.

Each green badge shows the aircraft, the airport, and the highest available pilot payout % there, color-coded on the rental spectrum. Hover a badge for the airport’s full info card and the 🛫 / 🛬 icons to set it as your departure or arrival in one click.

Airports that are off-screen get a badge pinned to the map edge with an arrow pointing their way — click it to pan there. And when your selected departure or arrival scrolls off-screen, its dashed line gets a badge at the edge too (cyan for the departure, orange for the arrival) so you always know where the line leads back to; click it to pan back.
🛩
Rent Your Aircraft from an Airport’s Owner
A route-planning shortcut (v3.53.0): once you’ve picked an aircraft in the Aircraft selector, every airport tooltip — and the departure/arrival cards in the distance matrix — shows a green 🛩 <type> for rent line telling you which of that airport owner’s airports have your aircraft available to rent right now (no ground-operations timer running). If the owner has none free, you’ll see a muted “none rentable from <owner> now” instead. It scans the owner’s entire fleet, so you know whether you can actually get your airframe before you commit to the route.

For an at-a-glance map view, open the 👁 Display menu and turn on Highlight other airports with <type> (the row carries your selected aircraft’s code). After you select a departure or arrival airport on the map, it drops a badge on the other airports owned by that airport’s owner where your aircraft is rentable now — pinpointing where to go pick it up. When no aircraft is selected, this row instead reads Select Aircraft and, when clicked, closes the map and opens the aircraft selector so you can choose one — the rest of the planner dims so you can focus on picking an aircraft (the dim is purely visual and clears as soon as you choose one, click outside, or press Esc).

A faint dotted green line links your selected airport to each of those rentable airports (styled so it won’t be confused with a route, measurement, or distance line). Departure and arrival are handled independently, so each draws its own lines and badges to its owner’s rental airports. If a rentable airport is off-screen, the line runs to the edge of the map and the badge waits there with an arrow pointing toward it — click or tap that edge badge to pan the map and center the airport. The edge badges keep working as you pan and zoom, even once the departure or arrival itself scrolls off-screen.

The badges act like airport dots: hover one to see the same airport tooltip a map dot shows (name, owner, payout, runways, scenery, weather, and rental availability). Hovering also reveals two quick-action icons — a take-off plane (🛫) on the left sets that airport as your departure, and a landing plane (🛬) on the right sets it as your arrival. On touch devices the two icons are always visible.
✈
Point-to-Point Distance
Type any two ICAO codes into the Departure and Arrival fields to instantly calculate the great-circle distance in nautical miles. The result appears to the right of the inputs. Fields turn red if an ICAO isn't found in the database. Press Enter or Tab to move between fields; Escape clears both.
🗺️
File on SimBrief
Once both departure and arrival ICAOs are valid, the File on SimBrief → button lights up. Click it to open SimBrief's dispatch page pre-filled with your route — ready to generate a full flight plan in one click.

Hover the button to preview exactly what will be sent: origin, destination, aircraft type (if one is selected), and flight number. The flight number is synthesized from the 3-letter owner codes of the departure and arrival airport owners.

Route pill on the map (v3.8.0+) — whenever a complete dep → arr route is set and the interactive map is open, a pill slides up from the bottom centre of the map showing the route, selected aircraft, and flight number (Flt: XXXXXX). Click  ✈ File on SimBrief → on the pill to file directly from the map screen without returning to the main planner. Click the DEP → ARR route text to swap departure and arrival in one click — the map breadcrumb, highlight, and main-screen fields all update together. Dismiss with ✕ — the pill reappears automatically if you change either endpoint.

Move the pill (v3.41.0+) — it sits at the bottom centre by default and automatically shifts left or right if that spot would cover your departure or arrival airport. Grab the ⠿ handle on its left edge to drag it anywhere over the map. Your chosen position is remembered for the rest of the session; reload the app to return to the default bottom-centre placement.

ⓘ Full airport stats from the map (v3.45.0+) — the ⓘ button on the pill pops up a panel just above it showing the complete departure and arrival airport cards side-by-side — owner, pilot payout, SimFly category & level, rotations remaining, runways, weather, and the aircraft-suitability verdict — the same cards that otherwise appear at the bottom of the main planner. The panel tracks the pill if you drag it and refreshes as you change the route; click ⓘ again, click off the panel, or press Esc to close it.

Stop-sign button color (v3.68.0+) — both the main button and the map pill recolor to show the health of the current route for the selected aircraft, so you get a go/no-go read at a glance:
• Green — good to go: the aircraft clears suitability at both ends (runway, range, category), the weather is VFR, rotations are available, and the aircraft isn’t stranded elsewhere in the owner’s fleet.
• Blue (glowing) — everything green and your selected aircraft is rentable right now at the departure airport; the button pulses and the tooltip says so.
• Amber — caution: 3 or fewer rotations left at an endpoint, non-VFR (MVFR/IFR) weather, strong or gusting surface wind (≥25 kt sustained or ≥30 kt gusts), a thin range margin (the route uses 85–100% of the aircraft’s range); the aircraft isn’t at your departure but IS at your arrival (the tooltip suggests swapping departure and arrival); or the aircraft isn’t at your departure but the departure or arrival owner has it at a third airport (other than this route’s departure or arrival). The tooltip says which owner (departure or arrival) and names that airport.
• Red — not recommended: the aircraft is unsuitable for the departure or arrival, or an endpoint has zero weekly rotations left.
If departure and arrival are the same airport the button is simply disabled (a zero-distance route can’t be filed), and if an airport has no runway data the hover adds a neutral “suitability unverified” note.
Hover the button to see exactly what drove the color — it names the specific tripped check (e.g. the exact runway shortfall or range overage, the weather’s limiting ceiling/visibility, or how many rotations remain), not just a generic “unsuitable.” Pick an aircraft in the panel for the suitability- and availability-based colors; without one, the color still reflects weather and rotations.
✈️
Plot SimBrief Flight Plan on the Map
Open the interactive map (the ⊙ Map button at the top of the Flight Planner) and click ✈ SimBrief Flight Plan in the header bar, just to the right of 📐 Measurement. The app calls SimBrief's public API for your most recent flight plan and draws the full route — origin, every navlog waypoint, and destination — as a polyline with waypoint dots. Hover the magenta origin or destination ring for the route summary (flight number, total distance, waypoint count, SimBrief route string).

The first click prompts once for your SimBrief Pilot ID — find it at simbrief.com → Account → Pilot ID. It's stored locally so subsequent clicks fetch instantly. Clicking the button while a flight plan is plotted clears it.

Right-click the button to open Flight Plan Settings. The modal lets you change:
  • SimBrief Pilot ID — replace the value you entered on first use.
  • Display waypoint labels (default On) — when on, each waypoint's ident is shown as a permanent label next to its dot. When off, idents only appear on hover (along with altitude, via-airway, and flight stage).
  • Route color (default magenta) — color picker + hex input. Applies to the polyline, waypoint dots, and origin / destination rings.
Settings are saved to localStorage and persist between sessions. If a flight plan is already plotted when you save, AA re-renders it in place with the new settings — no need to re-fetch.

Status feedback (fetching, success, errors) appears as a toast pill at the bottom-right of the window.
🎲
Random Flight
For when you just want to fly somewhere new. In the map's 🛠 Tools menu, 🎲 Create Random Route picks a random departure and a random destination at another pilot's SimFly-owned airport, draws the route, frames it on the map, and shows the File on SimBrief button so you can fly it immediately.

If you've already selected a departure airport (click a dot on the map), the item becomes 🎲 Choose Random Destination — it keeps your departure fixed and re-rolls only the destination each time you click. Clear the route (the ✕ on the route chip) to go back to fully random routes.

After your first roll, a 🎲 Re-roll button appears in the map header for one-click re-rolls with the same filters (right-click / long-press it to edit them). Click the small ✕ on its right to dismiss it; it returns the next time you create a random flight.

Right-click the menu item (long-press on touch) to set filters — they all stack, so combine as many as you like: restrict to airports currently shown on the map (honours every active map filter); airport categories (colour-coded pills) and continents; multi-select countries and owners (searchable, with All / None — the count beside each updates live to reflect your other filters); a distance range in nautical miles; and — only when the selected aircraft has flight-time calibration — a flight-time range in minutes. In Create Random Route mode a Departure / Arrival toggle lets you filter each end of the route independently. Category, continent and country options only list values that have an owned airport. Leave any field blank, list empty, or toggle off for no limit. The ↺ Reset button in the bottom-left clears every filter on both panels back to its default in one click — and saves that cleared state immediately, so it sticks even if you close the window without generating. Filters are saved in your browser, so the first use opens the filter dialog to set your preferences and every click after that generates a new flight instantly. Any existing route is always cleared first, so the map never shows more than one random route.
🔌
Live Aircraft Tracking (FSUIPC)
Open the interactive map and click 🔌 FSUIPC Live in the header bar. AA connects to Paul Henty's WebSocket Server running locally as part of FSUIPC7 and draws a rotating green plane icon at your aircraft's lat/lon, oriented to true heading. Hover the plane to see callsign, aircraft type, altitude, ground speed, heading, source label, and last-update age. Updates push every 500 ms (2 Hz) — smooth across a continental-scale map. The plane grays out automatically after 30 seconds without a fresh fix.

Auto-connect after a flight plan loads (v3.32.0) — once you plot a SimBrief flight plan, AA quietly checks for FSUIPC once a minute and connects on its own the moment FSUIPC7 + the WebSocket Server is reachable, so you don’t have to remember to click FSUIPC Live before alt-tabbing to the sim. The first time it can’t find FSUIPC you get a single heads-up toast, then it keeps checking silently. As of v3.39.0 it also re-checks the instant you switch back to the AA tab — so popping over to confirm your flight connects on the spot instead of waiting out the once-a-minute timer (which browsers throttle while the tab is in the background), and touchscreen flight-sim PCs are no longer mistaken for phones/tablets and skipped. Clicking FSUIPC Live yourself still connects instantly (and uses the faster 15-second reconnect if the link drops); stopping tracking, or clearing the flight plan, cancels the auto-checks.

Live button status dot:
  • Green dot — FSUIPC tracking is enabled and position data is being received (last update within 30 seconds).
  • Yellow dot — FSUIPC tracking is enabled but no position data has arrived recently (stale or connection interrupted). Resolves automatically when data resumes.
  • No dot — FSUIPC tracking is not enabled.

One-time setup:
  1. Install FSUIPC7 from fsuipc.com. The free unregistered tier is sufficient — position reads (latitude, longitude, altitude, heading, ground speed) are not paid features.
  2. During install (or via FSUIPC7's add-on manager) make sure Paul Henty's WebSocket Server add-on is enabled — it's bundled with FSUIPC7 and listens on ws://localhost:2048/fsuipc/.
  3. Run FSUIPC7 alongside MSFS. Load any flight (doesn't need to be moving).
  4. In AA's map header click 🔌 FSUIPC Live. The button switches to ⏹ FSUIPC Tracking… and within ~1 second your green plane appears.

Click the button again to stop. If the WebSocket can't reach localhost (FSUIPC not running, or the Henty add-on not enabled), AA shows an error toast and retries every 15 seconds — so MSFS restarts don't require re-clicking. While it's retrying after a drop the button reads ⏹ FSUIPC Reconnecting… (v3.39.0) rather than Tracking…, so you can tell at a glance it's re-establishing the link; it returns to ⏹ FSUIPC Tracking… the moment the link is back.

Auto-resume across reloads (v3.1.0) — if FSUIPC tracking was active when you refresh the tab (or the browser crashes and reopens), AA silently reconnects on the next page load and restores the aircraft marker, breadcrumb trail, and Follow A/C state exactly as they were — no need to re-click FSUIPC Live. The auto-reconnect is quiet (no connecting / retry toasts; a single confirmation toast on success) and gives up after 2 minutes if the sim can't be reached — so opening AA on a machine without FSUIPC (a laptop away from the sim PC) won't retry localhost forever. Explicitly clicking ⏹ FSUIPC Tracking… to stop clears the auto-resume intent, so a deliberate disconnect stays disconnected after a reload.

Why FSUIPC? No cloud round-trip, no API tokens, no handshakes that can silently fail, no SimFly infrastructure. Position data travels straight from your sim to your browser over loopback. Browsers exempt localhost from mixed-content blocking (W3C “potentially trustworthy” rule), so the local WebSocket works even though AA itself is served over HTTPS.

📍 Follow A/C button — while a live source (FSUIPC or STKP) is active, a 📍 Follow A/C button appears next to the source button. Click it to lock the map onto the aircraft — every position update smooth-pans the view (Leaflet's animated panTo, 400 ms tween) so the plane stays centred without jitter on either FSUIPC's 100 ms cadence or STKP's 10 s polling. Zoom is preserved; only the centre changes. Manually dragging the map turns Follow off automatically, so panning around to look at something never fights the auto-centre. As of v3.14.0 this also covers the two other map-navigation gestures: right-mouse drag (the universal map-pan affordance) and the Shift+drag zoom rectangle. State persists across sessions. As of v3.14.1 the same toggle is also available as a 📍 pushpin button on the data panel (immediately to the left of the ⚙ options menu) — handy when the Live menu is collapsed and you want to flip Follow on or off without opening it.

📍 Shift-click to pick an aircraft icon (v2.77.0+) — Shift-clicking the Follow A/C button opens a small picker with four silhouettes: Airliner, Prop, Helicopter, and Business Jet. Click any tile to switch the live-aircraft marker icon instantly — the change applies the moment you click and persists across sessions. All four icons are hand-rolled inline SVG so they stay crisp at every zoom and rotate cleanly to the live heading; the Helicopter uses a rotor-disc circle so it reads correctly at any heading.

Double-click to zoom in (v3.6.12) — double-clicking the aircraft icon centers the map on the aircraft and zooms to a 3 nm wide view (matching the airport double-click zoom). Follow mode is automatically turned off so the map stays at the zoomed position. Works for both FSUIPC and Demo sources.

Persistent breadcrumb trail — the green flight trail (fresh + archive tiers, up to ~10,000 nm coverage) autosaves to localStorage every 5 seconds while flying, so a browser close or crash mid-flight no longer loses your flown path. On the next page load the trail is re-rendered at map-init time. Manually stopping a live source also preserves the trail (it used to wipe on every stop) so switching FSUIPC → STKP, idle gaps, and reloads all keep the same flight's track visible. As of v2.75.1 the trail draws on its own dedicated map pane (z-index 670) so it sits above the SimBrief route line and waypoint markers — you can always see where the aircraft has actually been versus the planned route. The track auto-resets when a new flight starts — defined as a fresh SimBrief flight plan being loaded (a different time_generated than the last one we cleared against) AND the aircraft being on the ground (phase = ground, or GS < 5 kt, or GS < 30 kt and ALT < 1000 ft). Re-fetching the same SimBrief plan after a reload is a no-op, and loading a new plan mid-flight (e.g. diverting) also preserves the trail — only the unambiguous "new plan + on ground" combination wipes the breadcrumb.

SimBrief flight plan persists across reload — the most recently plotted plan is saved to localStorage on every successful fetch (v2.75.1+). On the next page load it re-renders silently at map-init time — the route line, origin/destination rings, waypoint markers, and any waypoint-alarm rings all reappear without re-clicking SimBrief Flight Plan. The reload-restore path skips the success toast and the auto-fit reframe so it doesn't override your last viewport. Clicking ✕ Clear Flight Plan wipes the cache.

Active alarm visuals persist across reload — geofence rings + labels and waypoint highlight rings now reappear automatically on the next page load (v2.75.1+). Alarms themselves already survived reloads in localStorage, but their map visuals didn't render because the map wasn't initialised when _afkInit ran; the visuals are now restored at map-init time as well.
🎬
Flight Demo Mode
For demos and presentations at locations where MSFS / FSUIPC connections are unavailable (corporate networks, kiosks, conference rooms that block flight-sim sites). Drives AA's telemetry display and the full alerting system with a synthetic flight along a saved plan — everything that lights up during a real FSUIPC flight lights up the same way during a demo.

Open from 🔌 Live → 🎬 Flight Demo Mode in the map header. The configuration modal has three sections:
  • Flight Plan — Save Demo Flight Plan writes the currently-loaded SimBrief plan to a JSON file (departure, destination, every waypoint as name + lat/lon). Load Demo Flight Plan reads one back from disk. Save is disabled until a flight plan is loaded.
  • Aircraft — pulldown lists every aircraft in the database, grouped by SimFly category (1 → 7) and sorted alphabetically within each. Defaults to your current AA aircraft pick, or a Cat 6 aircraft if none is selected.
  • Start — kicks off the demo with the selected aircraft and plan.
Per-category performance — the demo synthesizes climb / cruise / descent from the SimFly category, not per-airframe data: Cat 1 (Light GA) sits at 4,000 ft / 110 kt, Cat 5 (Bizjet) at FL350 / 380 kt, Cat 7 (Widebody) at FL380 / 470 kt. Short routes auto-scale the V-profile so the aircraft still reaches a sensible step-cruise altitude rather than spiking through it. A Top-of-Descent envelope (v3.6.16) also caps the climb to whatever the remaining track distance can still descend from, so even a very short hop tops out at a realistic altitude and flies a proper descent to landing instead of arriving high.

Runway selection & final approach (v3.0.0) — before starting, the demo fetches the current METAR for the destination airport from CheckWX and selects the runway with the highest headwind component. If weather data isn’t available within 4 seconds the runway is picked by best alignment with the route’s inbound bearing. A virtual Final Approach Fix (FAF) is inserted into the route plan 8 nm outbound from the selected landing threshold on the extended runway centerline, so the aircraft intercepts the final approach course and flies the last segment precisely aligned with the runway. The departure runway heading is held for the first 45 seconds after takeoff (v3.6.8; earlier builds used 90 s / 30 s / 2,000 ft AGL / 5 nm) so the track lines up with the scenery centerline on the takeoff roll and initial climb before it turns to follow the plan.

AP target vs live (v2.98.1) — the AP panel HDG / ALT / SPD readouts show the value the autopilot is chasing, not the current live integrator state. Managed = the auto-derived target (next-wpt bearing / VNAV alt / plan-baseline gs); Selected = the user setpoint. Live telemetry remains visible in the floating data panel.

Floating control bar — while the demo is running, a control bar appears at the bottom of the map with:
  • ⏸ Pause / ▶ Play and ⏮ Restart
  • Scrub slider — drag to jump to any point along the flight. The total flight time shown next to it refines as the flight progresses (v3.6.0), converging on a more accurate figure during cruise.
  • Phase label — current synthetic phase (GROUND / CLIMB / CRUISE / DESCENT / APPROACH)
  • Speed presets — 1× / 3× / 10× (v3.6.0), plus a free-slider for arbitrary 0.1× – 25× playback
  • 📡✖ Stale — stops emitting packets but keeps the marker. After 30 s the icon greys out and any configured Loss-of-Telemetry alarms trip per their thresholds. Click again to resume the stream.
  • ⛓✖ Disconnect — clears the live-aircraft marker entirely (mirrors a full FSUIPC drop). Click again to reconnect.
  • ✕ Exit — end the demo and clear the synthetic aircraft.
  • ⏷ / ⏶ Collapse / expand (v3.6.15) — the small toggle in the bottom-right corner of the bar hides all the playback controls and shrinks the bar to a compact pill so it stops covering the map; click again to bring the controls back. Works just like the telemetry panel's show/hide button, and the collapsed state is remembered across sessions.
Telemetry seam — the demo engine emits packets through the same _liveAcRenderMarker function the FSUIPC integration uses, so every downstream consumer (AFK alerts, data panel, breadcrumb trail, follow-aircraft mode, stale-check, mobile alerts) behaves identically to a real flight. The only thing that knows it's a demo is the engine itself.

Minimal kiosk lockdown — while the demo runs, the 🔌 FSUIPC Live, 🛩 STKP Live, and ✈ SimBrief Flight Plan buttons are disabled to prevent the marker getting yanked out from under the synthetic packets. Everything else — panning the map, opening airport tooltips, the alerts panel, the data panel — stays fully interactive so you can walk an audience through features during playback.
✈️
Aircraft Panel
Click the Aircraft header to expand the panel. Use the category tabs to filter by aircraft type, then click any chip to select an aircraft. The spec strip shows takeoff distance, landing distance, and range — both at full MTOW and adjusted for your current fuel and payload settings.

The Fuel slider sets fuel load as a percentage of max capacity. The Payload slider sets passenger/cargo load as a percentage of max payload. Both sliders feed into the adjusted performance column and affect the suitability check. The stacked Weights bar shows OEW, payload, and fuel as proportions of MTOW.

When an aircraft is selected, the Filter airports unsuitable for aircraft button appears below the matrix. Click it to dim any airport the selected aircraft cannot use — based on runway length, surface type (paved vs. unpaved), SimFly category limits, and whether the destination is within range from the current departure. The filter re-evaluates automatically whenever you change the departure airport or adjust the fuel or payload sliders.

Your selection is remembered between sessions — the aircraft, fuel%, and payload% are saved to localStorage the moment you change them, so the app opens with your last setup intact. Launching from the Recip Tracker (Plan Flight or Send N to AA) still overrides the saved state for that session.

Flight time calibration — the Flight time calibration panel near the bottom of the spec strip turns measured distances into estimated en-route times. Enter your shortest and longest flight in the selected airframe, each as a distance in nautical miles and an elapsed time in minutes (both read straight off the SimFly UI). The app fits a straight line through those two points to estimate time for any distance, and appends an H:MM est. figure to the ruler/measurement tooltip, the Region Mode hero line, and the selected route line — updating live as you drag the measurement or stretch the hero line toward the arrival. Calibration is saved per aircraft in localStorage; if the current aircraft has no calibration (or none is selected) the estimate is simply omitted.

Improve accuracy with live flight data (v3.63.0) — turn on the checkbox in the calibration panel and the app learns each aircraft’s real performance from the flights you actually fly with FSUIPC connected. On every flight it captures the airborne time (wheels-up to wheels-down, so taxi is excluded) and the actual distance flown, then blends those with your two reference flights to sharpen all the estimates above. If telemetry drops mid-flight (e.g. you open the in-sim menu) the time and distance both pause; a gap longer than five minutes discards that flight. Captured flights are grouped into ~100 nm distance bands — each band keeps your most recent flights — so repeated flights at one distance refine that point instead of skewing the line, and your spread of short and long legs is preserved. A finished flight is credited to your loaded SimBrief plan’s aircraft type, or to the selected aircraft when no plan is loaded. The setting is per aircraft and off by default; the View data button opens a graph of your captured flights with the calculated line drawn through them and a dated table where you can remove an individual flight or reset the aircraft’s live data. This feature requires a PC sim with FSUIPC, so it’s desktop-only.

Density-altitude correction — required runway distance is automatically scaled for density altitude using a per-category factor (light pistons feel DA the most, jets the least). When you've fetched weather, the airport's METAR temperature and altimeter setting drive the calculation; otherwise the app falls back to ISA conditions from the field elevation alone. The suitability tooltip shows the DA used and its source — e.g. DA 11,400 ft (METAR, 14:30Z) or DA 9,228 ft (ISA — fetch weather for accuracy). Cold or low-pressure airports never reduce required distance below book numbers.
ℹ️
Airport Info & External Links
Hover over any ICAO label — in the matrix, or in the airport name lines below the inputs — to see a popup with the airport's full name, IATA code, city, country, SimFly category and level, elevation, and runway details (lengths, surfaces, and lighting). Airports listed for sale show a 🏷 marker in the tooltip. To open a for-sale airport's SimFly user-marketplace listing, right-click the dot and click 🏷 View sale listing on SimFly in the Quick-Rate popover — or use the shortcut of double right-clicking the dot to jump straight there in a new tab.

The airport name lines are also clickable links to the SimFly airport details page. Small icon buttons beside each name open Flightsim.to and FS Addon Compare search results for that ICAO in a new tab. Tip — hold Shift to open these in the background.
⛅
Weather & Flight Conditions
The departure and arrival airports auto-fetch a METAR the moment you set or change either ICAO, and again whenever you return to the tab if their cached data is older than 30 minutes — so the route endpoints are always current without you doing anything. Click the ⛅ button in the top-left corner of the distance matrix to fetch live METAR data for every other airport currently in view in one batch. Each ICAO label gets a colored dot showing its flight category: VFR (green), MVFR (blue), IFR (red), or LIFR (magenta). Hover any dot to see the full decoded report — conditions, wind, visibility, ceiling, observation time, and raw METAR text.

If an airport has no reporting station of its own, the nearest one within 100 nm is used as a fallback, shown with a dashed ring around the dot. Weather data is cached for 30 minutes; switching owner dropdowns clears the cache and prompts a fresh fetch. If the Distance Map panel is open when you fetch, map marker rings update to show flight categories too. Distance Map info cards below the map also include a Weather section with the same decoded report whenever METAR data is cached for the highlighted route's airports.
📋
Owner Distance Matrix
Select a Departure Airport Owner and an Arrival Airport Owner from the dropdowns to build a full distance matrix — every departure airport on the rows, every arrival airport on the columns. The brightest yellow cell marks the longest route currently visible within the active distance filter range.

Click any distance cell to highlight its row and column and populate the Departure and Arrival fields above with those ICAOs.

The ⇄ button between the owner dropdowns swaps the Departure and Arrival selections instantly.
↕️
Row & Column Sorting
Every row and column header in the matrix has a small ⊙ pivot dot on its right side. Click the dot to sort the opposite axis by distance to that airport — clicking ⊙ on a departure row sorts the arrival columns by distance from that dep airport; clicking ⊙ on an arrival column sorts the departure rows by distance to that arr airport.

Each click on the same pivot dot advances the mode: ⊙ (alphabetical) → ▲ Nearest first → ▼ Farthest first → ⊙ (cleared). The active pivot dot is highlighted so you can always see which airport the sort is anchored to. Clicking the dot on a different airport on the same axis switches that pivot to the new airport.

The two axes sort independently. A row pivot and a column pivot can both be active at the same time — for example, arrival columns sorted by distance to one departure airport while departure rows are sorted by distance to one arrival airport. Because a ⊙ only ever reorders the opposite axis, the header you click never moves out from under your cursor.

Row and column highlighting is independent of sorting. Click a row label or column header to highlight it and populate the Departure / Arrival ICAO fields above — this does not change the sort. To restore alphabetical order, click each active pivot dot until it cycles back to ⊙.

Tip — hovering a pivot dot shows a tooltip describing what the next click will do (e.g. "Sort dep airports by distance to KSEA").
🎚️
Distance Filter
The dual-handle slider below the matrix filters routes by distance. Drag the left handle to set a minimum and the right handle to set a maximum. Routes outside the range are dimmed; routes within range remain fully visible. The longest route within the active range is highlighted in yellow. You can also drag the filled bar itself to pan the range window without changing its width.
🏷️
Category Filter
The numbered circle buttons (1–7) below the distance slider filter the matrix by SimFly airport category. They form two independent groups — Dep filters departure airports (matrix rows) and Arr filters arrival airports (matrix columns). All categories are shown by default. Click a circle to toggle that category off or on for its side — airports in hidden categories are dimmed in both their ICAO labels and their distance cells. All categories can be toggled off simultaneously.

The Inv button at the end of each group inverts that group's selection — active categories become inactive and vice versa, including toggling all off when all are on.

Enable Hide filtered airports (below the circles) to remove airports from the matrix entirely rather than dimming them. This applies to airports in hidden categories, and also to any row or column where every route falls outside the active distance filter range. Row and column highlights on hidden airports are cleared automatically.
▶
Departure / Arrival Info Panel
As soon as a departure or arrival airport is set — by typing an ICAO, clicking a matrix row / column, or selecting a dot on the interactive map — a panel appears beneath the matrix showing the full airport details for that endpoint. Each side renders independently: the departure card shows on the left, the arrival card on the right, and either card hides on its own when its ICAO is cleared. Cards include city, owner, SimFly category, elevation, runway data, aircraft-suitability verdict, and the latest cached METAR / weather summary when available — matching the matrix header tooltips so the information is in one persistent place instead of behind a hover.

Departure chaining (v3.61.0): when you set a new departure using the 🛫 icon on a rental badge while a departure is already selected, the previous departure is automatically moved into the arrival slot. So tapping 🛫 on one badge after another chains a route (the airport you just clicked becomes the departure, the one before it becomes the arrival). This applies only to the badge’s 🛫 icon — matrix row headers, the radial menu, plain map-dot clicks, and typed ICAOs keep their existing behavior. Arrival selections are never moved, and re-clicking the current departure does nothing.
⊙
Map
Click ⊙ Map in the panel header to open the interactive map. All SimFly player-owned airports are shown as color-coded dots (matching each airport’s SimFly category color). Hover any dot to see a tooltip with the ICAO, category, airport name, owner, and longest runway details after a brief delay. Once the map is open, click ⊙ Select Regions in its toolbar to start the cross-region distance matrix workflow (everything below describes that workflow).

Step 1 — Departure region: click and hold anywhere on the map to place the departure center, then drag outward to draw a circle. The radius line and live nm distance guide your selection. Release to lock the region. If an aircraft is selected in the main UI, a dashed gold ring shows its effective range from that center.

Step 2 — Arrival region: after releasing, the cursor switches to ruler mode — moving the mouse shows the distance from the departure center. Click and drag again to draw the arrival region circle the same way.

Cancelling mid-draw: right-click anywhere on the map during the region-drawing sequence (before or after the departure is locked, while drawing the arrival circle) to instantly cancel the operation and return to the explore state. Equivalent to clicking the Undo / Cancel button without having to move the cursor off the map.

Once both regions are set, a results bar appears with controls arranged around the centered route count. On the left: Sort order, a Dep Owners ▾ dropdown, and the Dep category filter group. On the right: the Arr category filter group, an Arr Owners ▾ dropdown, and the Max per region cap. The two owner menus filter independently — use Dep Owners to restrict departure rows and Arr Owners to restrict arrival columns. Each menu has a search box at the top: type any letters to instantly narrow the list to matching owners; press Enter to clear the search and return to the full list (selections are preserved). While search text is active, All and None apply only to the visible rows — hidden rows keep their current state. Use All or None with no search text to select or clear the complete list, or toggle individual owners by clicking their row. The matrix and map dots update in real time as you filter. If at least one owner is hidden, moving or resizing a region preserves that selection — hidden owners stay hidden, and any new owners entering the region appear visible by default. If all owners are visible, the list auto-refreshes when the region changes.

Map dot selection — click any airport dot on the map to build a route. Behavior depends on whether the matrix is visible: before both regions are drawn (or for clicks outside both region circles), it follows a fixed-departure anchor model — the first click sets the departure, the next click sets the arrival, subsequent clicks replace the arrival, and re-clicking the current departure clears the entire route. Once both regions are drawn and the matrix is visible, clicking inside the dep region always sets / changes the departure, and clicking inside the arr region always sets / changes the arrival — order doesn’t matter, so you can adjust either endpoint by clicking another in-region airport at any time. The selected route appears as a compact ✈ DEP → ARR pill in the breadcrumb strip above the map (next to the Flight Plan and Aircraft Track pills) — click its ✕ to reset the route, or click the pill itself to swap departure and arrival. Overlap airports (inside both circles) toggle to whichever slot they aren’t already in. Selecting a departure/arrival pair this way immediately updates the main screen departure and arrival fields and triggers owner auto-selection — no separate Send to Flight Planner click needed for that. Double-click any dot to zoom the map to that airport instead; double-clicking does not affect the route selection.

Unique Arrivals mode — the small Venn-icon button in the results bar (next to Max per region) is a three-state cycle: Off → ↗ Far → ↘ Near → Off. When active, the matrix keeps every departure row but hides any arrival column whose airport also appears in the departure region, so the grid shows only routes that land at airports unique to the arr region. Map dots are not affected — every airport in either region stays visible, so you can still route via dot clicks. While the mode is active, Max per region applies asymmetrically: the dep cap always keeps the N airports closest to the dep center, but the arr cap follows whichever direction is selected — ↗ Far (default on activation) keeps the N airports farthest from the arr center (favours the edge of the arr region where overlap with dep is least likely, so more truly-unique arrivals survive the column filter); ↘ Near keeps the N airports closest to the arr center instead. The current direction shows directly on the button as a coloured badge, and the tooltip explains what the next click will do. The icon shows an asymmetric Venn: dep circle outlined on the left, arr-minus-dep crescent filled in the arrival orange on the right when active. Below ~1500 px the “Unique Arrivals” label is hidden and only the Venn icon + direction badge show; the tooltip still explains the effect.

Crosses antimeridian — when a route, measurement, or dep→arr region distance crosses ±180° longitude (e.g. New Zealand to Hawaii), Leaflet draws the visual line the long way across the map even though the displayed distance is the short-way figure. In that case the on-map distance label shows a second row reading Crosses antimeridian, and the line itself switches to a dashed style so the apparent mismatch is explained at a glance.

Map category filter — the Map filter buttons at the top of the region screen also control the main screen departure and arrival category filters: toggling a category on the map instantly applies the same selection to both the Dep and Arr filter rows on the main screen.

Airport owner filter — the Airport Owners ▾ dropdown in the header bar filters which owners’ airports appear as dots on the map. It operates independently of the Dep Owners and Arr Owners controls (which affect only the matrix rows and columns) — use Airport Owners to declutter the map to just the pilots you care about while leaving the matrix unaffected. The panel behaves identically to the dep/arr owner menus: type to search, press Enter to clear the search, use All / None to bulk-select, arrow keys to navigate, and Space to toggle a highlighted row. Hovering the dropdown shows a color legend listing every owner currently in the filter with their assigned dot color.

Owner-colored map dots — the 🎨 button immediately to the right of the Airport Owners dropdown switches the map dots from category / payout colors to a unique per-owner color. The button is greyed out until at least one owner is selected; once enabled, click it to engage or disengage owner coloring. The mode chip in the filter strip (Category Colors (n) / Payout Colors (n)) extends to a three-way cycle when owner filtering is active: category → payout → owner → category. Up to 16 selected owners each get a maximally-distinct curated color; above 16 owners the assignment falls back to the hash-based palette used by the region-matrix owner stripes.

Sticky headers — departure row labels stay pinned to the left edge and arrival column headers stay pinned to the top as you scroll a large matrix, keeping airport identifiers visible at all times.

Owner stripe — each row and column header shows a thin colored ribbon for the airport owner. Click the ribbon to instantly filter the matrix to that owner only; a second click restores the full list.

Drag the thin handle between the map and the results bar to resize the map vs. matrix area. Click any matrix cell to select a route; the Send to Flight Planner → button closes the modal and populates the main fields.

Airport search — a search box floats in the top-left corner of the map. Type an ICAO code, airport name, city, country name, region / state / province name (California, Bavaria, Ontario, …), or continent name to find airports. Owner rows (⊙), continent rows (🌍), country rows (🌍), region rows (📍), scenery-developer rows (📦), and scenery-store rows (🛒) show Filter map → and filter the map to matching SimFly-owned airports. Selecting an individual airport row pins that airport as a visible dot — SimFly-owned airports appear in their normal category color; non-owned airports appear as a gray dot. Each pinned or filtered result appears as a chip in the filter status strip with a ✕ to clear it. After every search action the map zooms to fit every currently-visible airport — typing KSEA then +KPDX frames both pinned airports together; adding more pins or filters expands the view as needed.

Search by scenery store — the search box recognizes the marketplace your scenery was obtained from, as recorded in the Store field of your Scenery Library (SimMarket, Orbx Direct, MSFS Marketplace, …). A 🛒 store row filters the map to airports purchased there. Stores share the developer filter's behavior: combine several with +, exclude with -, and an active store filter shows as a chip in the filter strip — hover it for a per-store count of currently-visible airports. Only stores attached to at least one SimFly-owned airport in your library are offered.

Chain searches with commas — type a comma-separated list to run several searches in one go. For example California, United States, Europe, Kyndra & Africa previews each piece in the dropdown (showing what it resolved to) and, when you press Enter or click the ▶ Show all row, applies them all at once — the map shows the union of every item, exactly as though you'd searched each one in turn and added it with a +. Each segment can be any single-search term (ICAO, owner, country, region, continent, developer, store) or an owner @ place compound. Prefix the whole line with + to add the list on top of whatever is already shown, or with - to remove every listed item. The compound delimiters are @ and &, never the comma, so Kyndra & Africa stays one segment.

Additive search (“+” prefix) — prefix any search with + to accumulate results instead of replacing the current filter. Example: search Europe to filter to SimFly airports in Europe; then +Kyndra to also show Kyndra’s airports; then +California to also include California airports; then +Orbx to also include airports with Orbx scenery; then +SimMarket to also include airports bought from SimMarket; then +EGLL to also pin EGLL as a gray dot. A non-prefixed search clears all owner, country, region, continent, developer, store, and pinned filters and replaces them with the new result.

Negation search (“−” prefix) — prefix any search with − to exclude rather than include. −Germany hides all SimFly airports in Germany; −California hides all California airports; −Europe hides all European airports; −Kyndra removes Kyndra from the active owner filter or adds her to the excluded-owners set; −Orbx removes/excludes airports with Orbx scenery; −SimMarket removes/excludes airports bought from SimMarket; −EGLL removes EGLL from pinned or adds it to the excluded ICAOs set. Excluded items appear as red-tinted chips in the filter status strip with a ✕ to un-exclude.

Owner × location search (“@” or “&” delimiter) — find all of an owner’s SimFly airports inside a single state, province, region, country, or continent by joining the two with @ or & (the two delimiters are interchangeable). Examples: Kyndra @ Texas, Kyndra & Texas, Kyndra @ United States, Kyndra @ Europe, Kyndra @ TX, Kyndra @ EU. Order doesn’t matter — Texas @ Kyndra works exactly the same. Each result row shows the owner, the resolved place, and the airport count; clicking activates both filters at once. When a place name is ambiguous (e.g. Georgia = US state and country; Punjab = India and Pakistan; Washington = state and D.C.) the dropdown lists each interpretation with a parenthetical qualifier so you can pick the right one. The delimiter must have whitespace on both sides to be treated as a compound split or, if no spaces, the right side must not look like an email domain — so user123@hotmail.com still searches as a single owner name, but user123@hotmail.com @ Texas works as a compound query.

Stacking compound queries with + — prefixing a compound search with + appends it to the active compound filter instead of replacing it, so you can build a union of multiple (owner, place) intersections one query at a time. Start with Kyndra @ Europe, then add +jmribe @ Europe, then +CapitanCabal @ Europe — the map now shows all three pilots’ European airports together. Mix destinations the same way: Kyndra @ Europe then +Kyndra @ USA shows Kyndra’s European and US airports side by side. X’ing out an owner or location chip drops only the compound entries that referenced it; orphan chips (owners or places no longer in any compound entry) disappear automatically. A compound query without the + prefix wipes prior filters and starts fresh.

Removing a stacked compound with - — the exact inverse of + stacking. Prefix a compound query with - to drop the matching tuple from the active union. Example: after stacking the three Europe queries above, typing -jmribe @ Europe removes jmribe’s European entry and the map snaps to Kyndra + CapitanCabal in Europe. The dropdown automatically filters to show only currently-active compound entries when - is the prefix, so you can’t accidentally click a row that wouldn’t remove anything. Removing the last surviving tuple clears the compound filter entirely.

METAR on route assignment — clicking an airport dot to set the route departure or arrival automatically fetches that airport's current METAR (via the CheckWX API, with the same nearest-station fallback as the bulk ⛅ Get Weather button). When the data arrives, the airport's dot tooltip on the region map gains a compact weather row beneath the existing scenery / developer rows: a colored VFR / MVFR / IFR / LIFR pill, wind in DDD°/KKkt form, and visibility. Cached METARs (30-minute TTL) are reused, so re-selecting the same airport doesn't repeat the API call.

Route ICAO chips — when a departure and arrival airport are selected via dot-click routing, their ICAOs are shown in the header. Hover either chip to see the full airport tooltip: name, owner, category, elevation, runway details, and weather (if fetched). When an aircraft is selected, each chip’s tooltip also shows that airport’s suitability verdict for its role — takeoff for the departure, landing for the arrival, with the arrival adding an in-range / out-of-range check against the aircraft’s range — matching the main-screen matrix tooltips. Owned airports remain clickable SimFly detail links; non-owned airports show the tooltip but no link. The ⇄ button between the two chips swaps departure and arrival in one click — same handler updates the route line, matrix highlight, and main-screen ICAO inputs.

Adjust a drawn region — after both circles are down, drag a region’s center pin to move it, or drag its edge handle to resize it. While resizing, the live radius (“N nm”) floats next to the cursor (v3.6.3), the same readout shown during the initial draw.

Measurement — click 📐 Measurement in the header to activate the ruler. Click and drag anywhere on the map to draw a radius spoke (with tick marks at its 25%, 50% and 75% points) and range circle; the label shows distance, true bearing, and magnetic bearing (sourced from the NOAA WMM API). Distance units adapt to scale: at 20,000 ft and under the label shows feet and meters; above that threshold it shows nm and km (2 decimal places up to 100 nm, whole numbers above). If the drag reaches the edge of the map, the view auto-scrolls in that direction so the circle's radius can grow beyond the visible area. Release to freeze the measurement — it stays visible until explicitly cleared. Draw additional measurements; all stack simultaneously. A header toggle (shown whenever measurement mode is active) switches the display between ◯ Distance Ring — the full radius circle — and ╱ Distance Line — just the connecting lines, origin pins and labels; the button label always shows the current mode. Hiding the rings keeps a busy multi-segment route readable, and the setting applies to measurements drawn afterwards. Continuous-path routes — to measure a multi-stop path, finish a circle, then release the mouse and immediately click again: the new circle starts exactly where the last one ended, building a conjoined route leg by leg. The quick re-click is what continues the route; pause for a moment before clicking and the just-drawn circle behaves normally instead (its handles become available for resizing). Once a route has two or more legs, a distinct accent-bordered route total chip appears at its end showing the cumulative distance (Σ total, in both nm and km), the leg count, and the straight-line direct distance from start to end — so you can see how far the path deviates from a direct great-circle line. Saved measurements are movable and resizable: grab the origin pin (center handle) or the edge handle (the grip at the line's endpoint) to drag that point — the edge handle both resizes and rotates, distance and bearing following the cursor together. For a circle that belongs to a route, both handles act as waypoint movers: dragging one shifts the shared point so the adjacent legs follow and the route stays conjoined, with the total chip updating live. A standalone (non-route) measurement's center handle moves the whole circle. By default the circle drags rigidly, which means both its reported distance and its on-screen heading shift as you move it — that's map scale at work, and it gets pronounced for long lines (a long measurement moved toward higher latitudes can visibly swing by tens of degrees). Turn on the 🔓 Lock Distance toggle in the measurement toolbar and a relocated standalone measurement holds a constant true heading and its measured distance: it slides as a constant-heading line (a rhumb line — the straight line a navigator plots on a chart), so it never rotates no matter where you move it, at any latitude, and the distance label stays within a fraction of a percent of your original measurement. The Lock Distance setting is remembered across reloads and saved in your Master Save File; it applies to standalone measurements only (route legs always pivot around their shared waypoints). Resizing a locked measurement by its edge handle returns it to normal great-circle behaviour. Each measurement also has a ✕ close badge on the corner of its distance label — clicking it removes that circle. The badge rides the label, so it stays reachable even when the ring is hidden and never overlaps the line or handles. A standalone measurement simply disappears; deleting a circle from the middle of a route closes the gap automatically — the following leg re-anchors onto the previous leg's endpoint so the route stays one continuous chain with one fewer leg. Deleting the first or last leg of a route just trims that end. In measurement mode the cursor stays a crosshair, changing to a grab hand over an origin / move handle, a resize arrow over an edge handle, and a pointer over the close ✕ badge. The ✕ badge on each distance label is the per-measurement removal control; ✕ Clear All appears in the header whenever one or more measurements are on the map and wipes them all in one click. The distance / bearing label sits centered on the line for normal-length measurements, but auto-shifts below the line for short ones so the origin pin and edge handle stay fully visible. Starting a new region selection (⊙ Select Regions) automatically toggles measurement mode off so the two workflows don't compete for left-click — saved measurements stay on the map. A plain right-click on an empty part of the map also exits measurement mode (right-click drag still pans; right-clicking a dot still opens its radial menu).

Right-mouse drag the map to pan, in any phase or mode. Left-click is always owned by whatever workflow is active (region draw, measurement draw, dot click, handle drag); right-mouse drag is the universal pan affordance so you can navigate the map without exiting what you're doing. A pure right-click without dragging preserves its pre-existing meaning — opens the airport radial menu over an airport dot in explore / results phases (double right-click jumps straight to the Quick-Rate popover), cancels the in-progress region selection during region drawing, and exits measurement mode when made on an empty area of the map while measuring.

Use ↩ Undo to step back one phase (removes the last region drawn). Click ⊙ DONE (results phase only) to collapse the distance matrix and return to free map exploration — region circles stay on the map so you can still reference them. Click ⊙ Select Regions to start a fresh selection (this clears any previous circles). Use ✕ Close to exit Region Mode entirely. The main-screen ✕ Reset button also closes Region Mode and clears all region state.
✺
Airport Radial Menu (right-click a dot)
Right-click any airport dot on the map (or long-press on a touch screen) to open a labelled radial menu of everything you can do with that airport. The icons bloom outward from the dot, the rest of the map dims so the menu stays readable even in a busy region, and the clicked airport keeps a bright highlight ring. Click a spoke to open it; click anywhere else, press Esc, or zoom the map to dismiss.

The wheel adapts to each airport — only the actions that apply are shown:
• Airport info — a full card with stats, runways, weather (METAR), and, when an aircraft is selected, that airport’s takeoff / landing suitability. (always)
• Scenery / rate — opens the Quick-Rate popover to set the star rating, store, developer and notes. (always)
• SimFly details — opens the airport’s page on SimFly in a new tab. (owned airports)
• Airplanes — lists the rentable aircraft parked at that airport with payouts and live availability. (when rental data exists)
• Sale page — opens the airport’s SimFly sale listing in a new tab. (airports listed for sale)
• FS Addon Compare and flightsim.to — search that ICAO on each scenery site in a new tab. (always)

The clicked airport’s ICAO is shown in the centre of the wheel so you always know which airport you’re acting on. The four spokes that open a website (SimFly details, Sale page, flightsim.to, FS Addon Compare) open in a foreground tab on a normal click; right-click or middle-click one to open it in a background tab instead — the wheel stays up so you can fire off several at once (e.g. flightsim.to and FS Addon Compare) before dismissing it.

Fast path for rating: if you rate a lot of scenery, double right-click an airport to jump straight into the Quick-Rate popover, skipping the wheel. (This replaces the old “double right-click a for-sale airport to open its sale page” shortcut — the sale page is now a spoke, and double right-click is the universal rating shortcut.)
🗂
Map Layers (stacking order)
Open the 👁 Display menu and click 🗂 Layers to choose which map elements draw on top of which. The window lists every overlay — airport dots, airport tooltips, waypoint labels, the aircraft icon and trail, route line and labels, alert labels, and measurement/region overlays — each with a small graphic of the real element and its stacking value.

Reorder — drag a layer up (grab the ⋮⋮ handle) or use the ▲▼ buttons to nudge it one step. The top of the list draws on top of everything; the bottom draws underneath. Changes apply to the map instantly and are saved in your browser, so your chosen order is remembered between sessions.

This is the permanent way to settle any "which label covers which" question — e.g. if you'd rather a geofence alert tag sit above airport tooltips, just drag Alert labels above Airport tooltip. Layers can only be reordered among themselves: nothing can ever drop below the base map or rise above the menus and pop-up windows.

↺ Reset to Default at the bottom-left restores the original sensible order in one click, so you can experiment freely and undo any mess instantly.
🆕
New Airports
Airports added to SimFly within a configurable window (14 days by default) are highlighted on the map with a pulsing ring and a ✦ New Airport banner in their tooltip. The ring is a double radar-sweep animation that radiates outward from the dot — noticeable without being distracting.

The ring disappears automatically once the airport ages past your configured window — no manual upkeep needed. The date each airport first appeared is recorded by the hourly data pipeline.

Toggle visibility — open the 👁 Display menu and left-click ✦ Display New Airports to show or hide the indicators. The item only appears when new airports are present; a green tick on the right of the row marks it when active.

Display options — right-click ✦ Display New Airports to open the options panel. Choose the ring color (8 presets or a custom picker), pulse duration (1–30 s slider, labeled Fast ↔ Slow), and how many days an airport counts as new (1–365, drag up/down to scrub). A live preview inside the panel shows the pulsing ring updating in real time as you adjust settings. All settings are saved in browser storage; a Reset Defaults button restores the original 14-day, 6 s magenta configuration.
☼
Airport Level Badges
Every SimFly airport has a level in addition to its category. Category is the airport's fixed class (its size/importance); level is earned and climbs as the airport accumulates XP from traffic, raising its rotation capacity. Turn on level badges to see each airport's level at a glance as a ring of tick marks — a "corona" — radiating from its dot.

The marks are read like a clock: the first spike sits at 12 o'clock, and each additional level adds another evenly-spaced spike around the circle. Level 2 shows top + bottom, level 3 shows 12/4/8 o'clock, level 4 shows 12/3/6/9, and higher levels keep spreading the marks evenly. A thin white halo behind each mark keeps it legible on both the default and satellite basemaps. The spikes stay the same size at every zoom level.

Toggle visibility — open the 👁 Display menu and click ☼ Display Levels (on mobile, use the ☼ Levels button in the right-hand button band). Badges are on by default, and only level 2 and above are drawn — a single level 1 tick on every dot was more clutter than signal. Toggle them off any time; your choice is remembered between sessions. Level data refreshes automatically through the same hourly pipeline that feeds owner colors and categories.

Filter by level — right-click any of the Map category circles in the map header (long-press on touch) to open that category's level filter. The menu lists only the levels that real airports of that category currently have on the map under your other filters, each with a live count, so empty levels are never offered. Tick individual levels for a custom selection, or use the ≥ button beside any level to keep that level and everything above it. All restores every level; None hides the whole category. Each category keeps its own level selection. A small dot marks any circle that has a level filter applied, and a Level Filtering chip appears in the filter status strip — its ✕ clears all level filters, and ⊗ Clear All sweeps them up with everything else. Level filtering applies to map dots and the region distance matrix, just like the category filter.
🔄
Rotation Gauge & Filter
Every SimFly airport has a weekly pool of flight rotations — the number of times it can be flown into before its traffic resets for the week. The size of the pool (its capacity) grows with the airport's level. The rotation gauge shows, at a glance, how much of that pool is still available right now.

Each airport dot's outline doubles as a low-rotation warning. Because what matters for planning is whether an airport is running low this week, the outline only depletes in the danger zone: an airport with 10 or more rotations remaining shows a full 3 px black ring, and once it drops below 10 the ring shrinks anti-clockwise from 12 o'clock as a percentage of 10 (9 remaining ≈ 90% of the ring, 5 ≈ 50%, 1 ≈ 10%). An airport with zero rotations left has no outline at all — so the airports to avoid this week are simply the ones with no ring. The ring sits clear of the level-badge corona spikes, which radiate outside the dot.

Exact numbers — hover any airport dot and its tooltip shows the rotations remaining and total alongside its category and level: "ICAO  Cat n  Lvl n  (remaining / total)". The detailed airport tooltip and the right-click Quick-Rate card both carry a "Rotations: X / Y left this week" line as well, colour-coded green (10 or more left) / amber (fewer than 10) / red (none) so the count is available everywhere you inspect an airport.

Hover to enlarge — rest the mouse on any airport dot for half a second and it doubles in size, making the rotation ring and level-corona spikes easy to read on the airport you're examining. It returns to normal as soon as you move away.

Toggle the gauge — open the 👁 Display menu and click 🔄 Display Rotations, or simply middle-click anywhere on the map for a quick on/off. While it is on, a 🔄 Rotation Gauge chip also appears in the filter strip — click its ✕ to hide the gauge. It is off by default; once you turn it on your choice is remembered between sessions.

Rotations Left Only filter — also under 👁 Display, click 🔄 Rotations Left Only to hide every airport that has exhausted its rotations for the week, leaving just the airports you can still fly into. Airports with no rotation data are left visible. While the filter is active a removable 🔄 Rotations Left chip appears in the filter strip; its ✕ clears it, and ⊗ Clear All removes it along with every other filter. Rotation data refreshes automatically through the same hourly pipeline that feeds owner colours, levels, and categories.

Low-rotation warnings (3 or fewer left) — when a departure/arrival pair is selected, the on-map distance label adds a red ⚠ Low rotations note naming any endpoint with 3 or fewer rotations remaining, and a yellow ⚠ badge appears on the File on SimBrief buttons. Clicking File on SimBrief (the main button or the floating map route pill) then shows a confirmation first if the departure or arrival is at 3 or fewer — it lists the airport and its remaining count, and shows before the new-pilot-codes prompt. Cancel to back out, or File Anyway → to continue.
⬤
Scenery Library
The ⬤ Scenery split button in the map header controls your personal scenery library — a list of airports you have high-detail scenery for. Click the left area to cycle display modes: Highlight (black dot on each scenery airport), Only (filter the map to scenery airports only), and off. Click the ⚙ gear icon on the right to open the library panel. When the library has unsaved changes, a yellow numeric badge on the button shows the count of pending changes; both halves of the button show the count in their tooltip.

Adding airports from the map (fastest): Shift+click any airport dot at any time to instantly toggle it in or out of your library — no panel needed. Or click 📍 Add from map in the library footer to enter paint mode, where every dot click toggles scenery. A blue banner confirms the mode; click ✕ Done or press Escape to exit.

Folder scan (Chrome/Edge only): click 📂 Scan folder, point at your MSFS Community or X-Plane Custom Scenery folder, and the app detects airport codes from add-on subfolder names. A checkbox preview lets you confirm before importing.

Scenery config import (⋯ menu): import an X-Plane scenery_packs.ini or MSFS/P3D scenery.cfg directly — same checkbox preview as the folder scan. You can also drag and drop any of these files (or a .json backup) straight onto the modal.

FS Addon Compare import (⋯ menu): select a .har file captured from the FS Addon Compare website. Open FS Addon Compare → My Library in Chrome/Edge, open DevTools (F12) → Network tab, reload the page, then export and save as HAR.

FlightSim.to import (🛠 Manage menu → 🌐 Import from FlightSim.to): a guided two-step window that pulls in every airport you've downloaded from FlightSim.to — owned by a SimFly player or not. Step 1 provides a snippet (with a 📋 Copy snippet button); paste it into your browser's console (F12) while signed in to FlightSim.to and it saves a simfly-flightsimto-scenery.json file to your Downloads. Step 2 loads that file with 📂 Load JSON file. The import is fill-blanks-only: new airports are added in full, but for airports already in your library it only sets a Store, Developer, or Rating that's currently empty — your hand-set ratings and attributions are never overwritten, so it's safe to re-run whenever you buy more. Imported rows float to the top for review and a toast summarises the result.

Star ratings — every airport in the library has a 0–5 star rating shown next to its name. Click the left half of a star for a half rating, the right half for a full rating; click the same half again to clear back to zero. Ratings are saved automatically and round-trip through Save Scenery Library / Load Scenery Library. When a rating is set, it is also displayed in the airport’s tooltip on the region map.

Rate from the map — right-click any airport dot on the region map (or long-press for ~½ second on touch devices) to open a Quick-Rate popover anchored to that dot. Click stars to set the rating without leaving the map. If the airport is not yet in your Scenery Library, setting a rating adds it automatically (and turns on the Highlight overlay so the black scenery dot appears right away). Close the popover with ×, Esc, by clicking elsewhere on the map, or by zooming.

Keyboard entry (v3.40.0) — the popover is fully keyboard-driven for fast data entry. It opens with the rating focused, so ←/→ (or ↑/↓) adjust by half a star, 1–5 set a rating outright, and 0 clears it. Tab walks Rating → Store → Developer → Notes → links and cycles within the popover; on Store / Developer, type a few letters and press Tab to autocomplete to the top matching suggestion (the native ↑/↓ dropdown works too). Map pan/zoom keys are suppressed while a field is focused, and Esc closes from anywhere inside.

The popover also shows Store and Developer fields you can fill in directly — the same fields found in the Scenery Library panel, with the same autocomplete suggestions. Setting a value here is identical to editing the row inside the library. A collapsible ▶ Notes field is here too — click the triangle to expand it and jot a free-text note (up to 1,024 characters); a ● beside the label marks airports that already have one. When a note already exists, the popover opens with it expanded so you see it without a click. When any scenery changes are pending (from a rating, store, developer, or anything else), a small 💾 icon appears in the popover header; clicking it exports the full scenery database to a JSON backup file, the same as Save inside the panel.

For SimFly-owned airports, the ICAO code and name in the popover header are a clickable link to that airport’s SimFly details page. Two external links at the bottom of every popover — View on flightsim.to and View on FS Addon Compare — let you look up the airport’s scenery add-ons without leaving the app.

Sort and filter — the Show dropdown filters the list to All airports, SimFly-owned only (airports still owned by an active SimFly player), Rated only, or Unrated only. The Sort dropdown orders the list by ICAO, rating (high to low), or airport name. The text box above searches by ICAO, addon name, full airport name, and — as of v3.64.0 — the Store, Developer, and Notes fields too, so you can filter the list by a publisher, marketplace, or any word in a note.

Store and Developer attribution — each library row shows two values beneath the ICAO line: Store (the marketplace where the scenery was purchased) and Dev (the publisher / studio). Click a value to edit it (it turns into a text field); Enter or clicking away saves, Tab hops to the next field, and Esc cancels. Editing autocompletes from a built-in list of common marketplaces and developers plus any custom values you've already typed on other rows (type a few letters and press Tab to accept the top match). Both values are persisted to local storage and round-trip through Export / Import JSON. (v3.49.0: these — and Notes — are shown as plain text until clicked, instead of always-on input boxes.
