With a large library that meant ~2,000 text fields on screen, which some browser extensions re-scan on every keystroke and made typing lag; rendering them on demand keeps editing fast regardless of your extensions.)

Notes — each library row also has a collapsible ▶ Notes disclosure beneath the Store / Dev inputs (kept minimised until you click the triangle). Expand it to record a free-text note of up to 1,024 characters — install quirks, version numbers, reminders — with a live character counter. Notes are saved to local storage and round-trip through Save / Load Scenery Library. The same field appears in the right-click Quick-Rate popover.

Developer filter — the new Developer dropdown next to Show / Sort narrows the modal list to a single developer. Selecting a developer also filters the region map: library airports whose developer doesn't match are hidden, airports not in the library are unaffected. An active filter shows as a Developer: <name> chip in the region-map filter strip with a ✕ to clear it.

🛠 Manage Values modal — click the 🛠 Manage Values button in the Scenery Library footer to open a bulk-edit view of every Store and Developer value in your library. Each value shows the number of airports using it. Edit a value and click Save to rename it on every affected airport (rename to the exact spelling of an existing value to merge duplicates — handy for fixing typos like iniBilds → iniBuilds, or normalising spacing like Fly Tampa → FlyTampa). The ✕ button clears the value from every airport that uses it, after a confirm prompt that reports exactly how many airports will be affected.

⚙ Developers modal — click the ⚙ Developers button in the Scenery Library footer to open a per-developer summary of your library. The modal lists every developer for whom you have at least one rated airport and shows: total airports, rated count, and the average star rating over rated entries (displayed at two-decimal precision, e.g. 3.75/5, with a proportionally-filled star widget). Unrated airports are excluded from the average so they don't pull the developer's rating down. A search box at the top narrows the list to matching developers as you type — handy for finding one in a long list. Click any column header to sort. Click a developer row to expand or collapse their airport list; click the 🔍 button next to a developer's name to filter the Scenery Library to just that developer's airports. The 🔍 drill clears any leftover library search text and resets the rated / unrated / owned mode filter first, so you always land on a clean list of that developer's airports rather than an unexpected intersection of filters.

The library is stored in your browser’s localStorage. Closing the panel after making changes prompts you to 💾 Download & Close to save a backup. Use 💾 Save Scenery Library (⋯ menu) to back up or transfer to another machine at any time; 📂 Load Scenery Library reads a backup file back in (merging into your existing entries). When you open the modal with unsaved changes, airports you touched this session (added, rated, or had their store / developer edited) float to the top of the list under the current sort — so the rows you just shift-clicked or right-click-rated on the map are right where you need them to fill in store / developer metadata.
⭐
Scenery Star Rating Filter
⭐ Star Rating lives in the 👁 Display menu (on mobile, the ⭐ Rating button in the right-hand button band). It opens the Scenery Rating filter panel. Hover over the five stars to preview a threshold, then click to set it — airports in the Scenery Library with a rating below that value are hidden on the map and removed from the distance matrix. Click the same star again to clear the filter. A green tick marks the menu row while the filter is on.

The filter only applies to airports that are in your Scenery Library with a rating set — airports with no rating entry are always hidden when any threshold is active. If the Pilot Payout filter is also open, the Scenery Rating panel sits directly beneath it. Airport ratings are set via right-click (or long-press on touch) on any map dot.

Closing the panel keeps the filter on — clicking the panel's ✕ just hides the panel; the rating threshold stays applied to the map and matrix. To actually turn the filter off, click the Rating ≥ N stars chip in the breadcrumb strip. Clicking ⭐ Star Rating again re-opens the panel without changing the filter.

Move it out of the way (v3.46.0). Grab the panel's title bar — the ⠿ grip next to SCENERY RATING — to drag it anywhere on the map. It stays where you put it for the rest of the session and returns to its default top-right spot on reload.
💰
Pilot Payout Map
💰 Payout Colors in the 👁 Display menu (on mobile, the 💰 Payout button in the right-hand button band) switches the map to payout-percentage coloring. A green tick marks the menu row while the mode is on. The scale is anchored so 50% = yellow and 60% = green, making those two values immediately distinguishable at a glance. Below that: 1–20% runs red through orange-red (the non-premium range, capped at 20%), 20–40% runs orange through amber, and 40–50% transitions from amber into yellow. Grey dots indicate airports with no payout data available.

A legend in the lower-left corner of the map shows the gradient scale while the mode is active. Click the button again to return to standard category/owner coloring.

Closing the legend keeps payout coloring on — clicking the legend's ✕ just hides the legend; the map keeps coloring dots by payout. To actually turn payout mode off, click the Payout Colors chip in the breadcrumb strip (or the 💰 Payout button again with the legend visible). Clicking 💰 Payout while the legend is hidden re-opens it without flipping the mode.

Quick toggle for 0% airports — click the gray dot on the legend to switch the filter between 0% (show every airport) and 1% (hide airports with no payout set). When the filter is active the dot dims and gains a yellow inset ring as a visible state cue. The slider can still be used for any other threshold above 1%.

Payout data is imported via the SimFly KML Generator (v2.20.3+) using the Pilot Payout Data panel. The KML Generator stores it in your browser’s localStorage under the key simfly_payout_db, which this app reads automatically on load. Payout percentages are also shown in airport tooltips (after the owner name) whenever data is available.

Custom palette (v2.98.0). Right-click the gradient bar in the legend — or right-click 💰 Payout Colors… in the 👁 Display menu — to open a small palette editor. Place 2–5 color stops at any percentages you want; each stop has a native color picker and a % position field. The change applies instantly to the legend bar, the map dots, and the matrix, and persists in localStorage (key simfly_aa_payout_palette) so it survives across sessions. A Reset button restores the original red → orange → amber → yellow → green default. The 0% no-data grey is fixed and is not a user stop. (v3.46.0: opening it from the menu now drops the editor in its normal top-right spot, and dragging in the color picker stays smooth because the map repaint is batched to one per frame.)

Drag-to-position (v2.98.2). Each swatch in the editor is draggable — drop it anywhere on the preview gradient and the stop's % snaps to that x-position. Rows auto-sort low → high after each drop. Clicking a swatch (without dragging) still opens the native color picker.

Tick bars are draggable too (v2.98.3). Each tick on the preview gradient is a grab-target in its own right — mousedown and drag horizontally to reposition the stop directly. The colored chevron above each tick is the stop's own color so overlapping ticks stay distinguishable. The active drag lifts above siblings (gold outline + z-index) and re-sorts only on release. Maximum stops raised to 10 (was 5).
⠿
Movable Map Panels
(v3.46.0) The floating panels on the region map can be dragged out of the way, just like the File on SimBrief route chip. Look for the ⠿ grip on the title bar and drag from there. This works on the Pilot Payout legend, the Scenery Rating panel, the Payout Palette editor, the airport right-click rating popover, and the ⓘ airport-stats panel attached to the File-on-SimBrief chip.

Each panel remembers where you left it for the rest of the session, so reopening it brings it back to your chosen spot. Positions are not saved permanently — a page reload returns every panel to its default location.
🔖
Filter Status Strip
The row of chips between the 📐 Measurement button and the category circles gives you an at-a-glance summary of every active filter on the region map.

⊗ Clear All (v2.98.0) — appears at the front of the strip whenever any filter is applied or a SimBrief flight plan / aircraft track is on the map (v3.25.0). One click removes every applied filter at once (payout threshold, scenery rating, category filter, level filter, For Sale filter, owner / country / region / continent / developer / store includes, exclusion sets, pinned ICAOs) and wipes any plotted flight plan and recorded aircraft track — a single "reset the map" control. It returns the strip to its two-default-chip state: color mode + scenery indicator. The two display-mode chips stay because they're not filters. The confirmation toast names exactly what was cleared.

Display Mode — always visible; shows Category Colors or Payout Colors depending on the current color mode. Click the chip to toggle between the two modes.

Category Filtering — appears when at least one of the seven map categories is hidden. Click × to restore all categories.

🔄 Rotation Gauge — appears whenever the rotation gauge is being shown on airport dots. Click × to hide the gauge (you can also middle-click the map to toggle it).

🏷 For Sale — appears when the 🏷 For Sale Only toggle in the 👁 Display menu is on, restricting the map (and matrix) to airports currently listed for sale. Click × to turn it off.

N Owners Displayed — appears when the Airport Owners filter is active and shows how many owners’ airports are currently visible. Click × to show all owners.

Pilot Payout ≥ N% — appears when Payout mode is on and a minimum payout threshold is set above 0%. Click × to clear the threshold (the mode stays on).

Rating ≥ N stars — appears when the Scenery Rating filter is active with a threshold above zero. Click × to turn the filter off entirely.

✈ Flight Plan / Aircraft Track (v3.5.0) — two chips at the end of the strip. The ✈ Flight Plan chip appears whenever a SimBrief flight plan is plotted; the green Aircraft Track chip appears whenever a live breadcrumb trail is on the map. Click the chip body (v3.6.1) to zoom the map so that route or track fills about 90% of the viewport; click the × to clear that layer in one step — the same as the clear actions in the 🔌 Live menu, but reachable without opening it. They appear / disappear automatically as the plan and track come and go.

The strip truncates automatically if it would overlap the category circles, always leaving at least 30 px of clearance.
🔔
Alerts
Get notified by a loud, looping alarm when key events happen during long flights while you’re away from the keyboard. Click 🔔 Alerts in the map header to open the configuration modal. The left column lists five independent alarm types you can mix and match for a single flight; each one you add shows up as a card on the right with its own enable toggle, sound selection, and per-type settings. Hover any alarm icon or title in a card for a plain-English description of what it does and when it fires.

The six alarm types:
  • ⏰ Timer — a simple clock / countdown timer, independent of aircraft position. Two modes: Countdown (set hours / minutes / seconds; alarm fires after that delay) or At specific time (native time picker for the local clock; if past today, fires tomorrow). Useful for "wake me in 30 minutes" or "alarm me at 14:30". The card readout shows the absolute target time AND the live remaining countdown. Survives a page reload — if you set a Timer for 14:30 and refresh the page at 14:25, it still fires at 14:30. Repeat (v3.18.0, Countdown mode only) — when the Repeat toggle is on, the alarm auto-dismisses and reschedules after each firing so it repeats at the same interval indefinitely — no manual reset needed. Each cycle plays the full audio/visual alert before auto-dismissing after ~5 seconds. Turn off Repeat (or disable the alarm) to stop the cycle.
  • ⊙ Geofence — clicking this closes the modal, hands you a crosshair cursor, and shows a banner across the top of the map. Click and drag on the map to draw a circle (radius shown live in nautical miles), release to confirm, then pick a label, trigger (ENTER, EXIT, or both), and sound in the sub-modal that appears. The orange circle stays visible on the map and re-renders across sessions.
  • 📍 Waypoint — trips at the moment your aircraft passes a chosen waypoint in the loaded SimBrief flight plan. Uses closest-point-of-approach (CPA) detection, so it fires even when your track is a few nm off the planned line. Specifically: it watches the distance to the next un-tripped waypoint each tick, and fires when that distance starts increasing again (the CPA moment) — provided the closest approach was within 5 nm. Skipped while on the ground (so taxi doesn't trip it), resets on sim restart / new flight load, and cascades tight clusters (two waypoints sitting within 5 nm of each other fire on the same pass). Scope picker: Every waypoint, TOC (top of climb — first cruise-stage fix), TOD (top of descent — first descent-stage fix after cruise), or Specific waypoints (multi-select checkbox list). Selected waypoints get a cyan ring overlay on the route plot so you can see where they are.

    Shortcut: right-click any waypoint dot on the plotted route to add a one-off alarm for just that fix — a small modal opens with the ident, position, and a sound picker.
  • ─ Cruise Reached — one-shot alarm that fires when the aircraft reaches cruise. Two explicit modes (Mode dropdown on the card, v2.90.5+): Adaptive — the default; uses the phase detector (v3.16.0+: fires when the 5-second windowed VS settles within |VS| ≤ 200 fpm from a prior CLIMB phase; previously a variable deadband sustained for 35 s). At specific altitude — switches the trigger to a climbing-through-altitude crossing (accepts 35000, 35,000, or FL350); the NEXT ALERT countdown also shows live time-to-cruise computed from current altitude and vertical speed. v2.87.0: gated on confirmed wheels-up (sustained GS > 40 kt for 10 s) so it cannot fire while still on the ground at high-elevation airports. v2.87.1: a second structural gate requires peakClimbVS > +200 fpm observed in this session before the climb → cruise transition can happen — so sustained-level ground operations can’t false-trigger cruise. v3.16.0: if you re-enable or add a new Cruise Reached alarm while the aircraft is already in cruise phase, it waits silently for the aircraft to leave cruise and re-enter before firing — guaranteeing it always fires on a genuine new cruise entry rather than the current one.

    Only above (v3.16.0+) — an optional “Only above” altitude field appears on every Cruise Reached card. When set, the alarm skips cruise transitions detected at or below that altitude. This lets you filter out ATC-assigned climb-and-maintain altitudes (e.g. “climb and maintain FL240”) so the alarm only fires when the aircraft actually settles at your true cruise altitude. Accepts feet or FL form; leave blank to fire at any altitude.

    Step climbs (v2.90.5+) — the Cruise Reached alarm is one-shot per flight, so a single alarm cannot re-fire when you level off after a step climb. To handle step climbs, add one Cruise Reached alarm per level-off altitude in At specific altitude mode — e.g. set three alarms at FL210, FL280, and FL380 for a stepped trans-oceanic profile. Each carries its own crossing-detection state, so each fires independently as the aircraft climbs through its threshold. Dismissing one auto-disables only that one; the others stay armed for the next step. Combine with per-alert names (v2.89.0+) like “Initial cruise — FL210” / “Step climb 1 — FL280” / “Step climb 2 — FL380” to keep them straight in the trip overlay, the Alert History log, and on map pins. Leave them ungrouped — group cascade would defeat the per-step alerting.
  • ↘ Descent Started — one-shot alarm that fires when sustained descent begins from cruise. v3.18.2+: phase detector promotes to descent when the 30-second trend VS reads < -400 fpm (a wider trend window and a higher threshold than the cruise-entry path uses — extended turbulence with the autopilot engaged cannot fake a sustained 30 s, 200 ft net altitude loss; v3.16.0 introduced the asymmetric two-window model at 15 s / ±200 fpm and v3.18.2 widened both to suppress false TOD calls). Same wheels-up gate as Cruise (v2.87.0+). v3.16.0: if you re-enable or add a new Descent Started alarm while the aircraft is already in descent phase, it waits silently for the aircraft to leave descent and re-enter before firing — mirroring the Cruise Reached re-arm guard.
  • ↕ Altitude — trips when your aircraft crosses through an altitude you set. Pick Descending through or Climbing through; the alarm only fires when you actually cross the threshold in the chosen direction. It will NOT fire if you arm the alarm already on the “wrong” side — e.g. if you arm a Descending through FL100 alarm while you're at FL080, it waits for you to climb above FL100 and then descend back through. The input box accepts both plain feet (e.g. 10000 or 10,000) and flight levels (e.g. FL350, FL 200). The hint next to the input echoes the parsed value as you type so you can see how it was interpreted. v2.87.0: Altitude alarms now auto-rearm when the aircraft returns to the ground (same behaviour as Cruise / Descent), so a single armed alarm can fire across multiple consecutive flights without manually toggling it off and on between segments.
  • 📡 Loss of Telemetry (CTD) (v2.90.0+, seconds unit added v2.91.0) — your “did MSFS just crash?” canary. Fires when all three conditions hold simultaneously: FSUIPC Live tracking is ON (with no live source the alarm sits quiet); the aircraft is airborne (any phase except ground); AND no fresh telemetry update has arrived for the configured timeout (default 5 minutes, range 10 s – 60 min via the minutes / seconds unit picker). The 5-minute default is sized generously to tolerate normal MSFS pause-menu trips — opening Map / ATC / Settings (which pauses the sim and freezes FSUIPC pushes) will not false-alarm. Catches the situations where the green aircraft icon would otherwise just go stale silently with no audio cue: MSFS crash-to-desktop, MSFS freeze / hang, FSUIPC server crash, the user accidentally closing FSUIPC mid-flight. Why a special watchdog? The regular alarm tick runs inside the live-aircraft fix-arrival path — a real CTD freezes those fixes, which would freeze the very loop that could detect the freeze. This alarm has a separate 15-second watchdog interval that keeps running independent of fix arrivals, so it can still fire when the telemetry stream is the thing that went silent. Note: the 15 s watchdog interval also sets a floor on detection latency — a 15 s threshold actually trips somewhere between 15 s and 30 s after onset. Re-arms automatically on the next ground transition.
  • 🧩 Conditional (v2.91.0+) — fire when a custom combination of live telemetry parameters is met. Each alarm carries one or more condition rows; each row picks a parameter, a comparator (> or <), and a threshold. The combinator (top of the editor) chooses AND (every enabled row must currently be met) or OR (any one enabled row met fires). Available parameters: Altitude (MSL), IAS, GS, VS (signed fpm), Distance to destination, Distance to waypoint (with a waypoint picker, requires a SimBrief plan), Track miles remaining, Track deviation (v3.6.2 — nm abeam the SimBrief route, default 10 nm), Remaining time, Elapsed time, Alert ETA. Time-based fields accept seconds (3600), H:MM:SS / M:SS, or H/M/S token form (1h 30m 22s); altitude accepts feet or FL. Per-row checkbox lets you disable conditions without removing them so you can iterate without losing your setup. New rows are added in the disabled state (v2.92.3+) — configure the parameter, comparator and threshold first, then toggle the row on. This makes it safe to build a conditional alarm mid-flight without it firing the instant you add a row whose default threshold happens to be satisfied by current telemetry. One-shot per flight — fires once, auto-disables on dismiss, auto-rearms on next ground transition. Example: "alert me when GS > 400 kt AND altitude > FL350 AND elapsed > 1h 15m".
Sounds — five synthesized tones (Digital Clock, Klaxon, Soft Chime, Urgent Beeper, Gentle Bell) picked per alarm. Click the ▶ button in any card or the geofence sub-modal to preview without arming.

Sound duration — each alarm has a Sound for picker: Auto-dismiss after… a seconds value (the sound auto-stops after the chosen interval and the alarm is dismissed automatically) OR Indefinite (until dismissed) (the sound loops until you click DISMISS ALL or the ✕ on the trip overlay row). v3.3.0: new alarms now default to Auto-dismiss after 10 seconds (or your saved preference) instead of indefinite, so an alarm you miss stops on its own; switch any card to Indefinite if you want it to keep sounding. If you change the seconds value, it’s saved as your default for future new alarms. The Sound for picker also appears on the Geofence and Waypoint quick-add modals so you can set the duration up-front without revisiting the main Alerts modal afterwards.

Send to phone from the quick-add modals (v3.3.0) — the Geofence and Add Waypoint Alarm pop-ups now carry a Send to phone checkbox, so you can forward those alarms to your phone at creation time instead of opening the alarm card afterward. The checkbox only appears once a Mobile Alerts provider is configured (Live → Mobile Alerts).

Drag any number to scrub it (v3.3.0) — every numeric field in the Alerts panel and the Geofence / Waypoint pop-ups (timer h/m/s, geofence radius, auto-dismiss seconds, telemetry-loss threshold, altitudes) now supports the same click-and-drag gesture: press on the field and drag up to increase or down to decrease (drag farther to move faster). A plain click still focuses the field for keyboard entry.

Keyboard shortcut — on the per-geofence sub-modal and per-waypoint quick-add modal, press Enter to commit the alarm without reaching for the Add Alarm button.

Reorder & prioritize — grab the ⋮⋮ handle on the far left of any alarm card and drag it up or down to re-rank. The expand/collapse chevron sits immediately after the drag handle (▶ collapsed, ▼ expanded); the enable toggle and ✕ delete button stay on the right.

List order IS sound priority. There is no separate priority number — the alarm closest to the TOP of the list wins the audio channel when several alarms are tripped at once. Implications:
  • If you care more about hearing certain alarms than others (e.g. 📡 Loss of Telemetry should always override a quiet timer), drag those alarms to the top of the list.
  • Sounds never overlap — only one plays at a time. Additional alarms that pile on are still added to the trip overlay list visually; their sound simply waits in queue.
  • Dismissing the leading alarm via the ✕ on its row in the trip overlay seamlessly swaps audio to the next-highest still-tripped alarm.
  • List order also drives Save / Load file ordering and the on-card per-alarm sequence in the trip overlay, so reordering helps with visual scanning too.

Auto-disable on dismiss (v2.76.0+) — whenever an alarm fires and you dismiss it (manually via DISMISS ALL / ✕, or via the seconds-mode auto-dismiss), its enable toggle flips off automatically. Re-arming for the next use is now an explicit user action: click the toggle on the card. The Alerts toolbar badge counts only enabled alarms, so it visibly decrements as each alarm goes off — you always know at a glance how many alarms are still armed.

⛓ Alert Groups (v2.83.0+) — bundle 2 or more related alarms so the first one to fire is treated as the answer for the whole group. Every other member of the group is prevented from firing (v2.87.1+) and visually disabled in the same tick. When the user dismisses the firing member — or it auto-dismisses via its duration timer — the cascade then propagates: every member's audio stops, the trip overlay clears for the whole group, and every member's enable toggle is left in the off position. The "one fires, rest go silent" contract is now an enforcement rather than an aspiration: any sibling whose own conditions later satisfy still cannot fire while the parent is tripped, so duplicate Mobile Alerts pushes for the same logical event are no longer possible. Create a group three ways: (1) drag one alarm onto the MIDDLE of another (a green "+ Group" indicator shows the drop target — the top/bottom 30% of each card still reorders as before); (2) right-click any alarm for a context menu with New group with…, Add to existing group…, Remove from group, Rename group, Ungroup; (3) Shift-click multiple alarms to multi-select, then click the ⛓ Group N Selected button in the modal footer. Add an alert to an existing group by dragging its card onto the group’s header row (the row with the group name and mode pill) — the header highlights green with “+ Add to group” as you hover. This works even when the group is collapsed. Groups can be named (click the name label at the top of the group), collapsed as a unit (chevron next to the name), and members can be dragged in or out at any time. Reorder a whole group (v3.31.0) — grab the ⋮⋮ grip on the far left of the group header and drag it up or down to move every member together (their internal order is preserved), including onto the top / bottom drop zones. Groups auto-dissolve when membership drops below 2.

Group mode — three options (v2.91.0 added CASCADE/AND; v2.92.0 expanded to three explicit modes) — a pill on the group header (default CASCADE) cycles through the three modes on click:
  • CASCADE — the pre-v2.91.0 behaviour. The first member to trip fires the user-visible alert and every other member is auto-dismissed and disabled. One acknowledgement covers the whole bundle. Best for "any one of these is a problem, alert me about it" scenarios.
  • CONCURRENT (renamed from AND in v2.92.0; semantics reworked in v2.95.0) — the group fires its single composite alert at the moment every enabled member is currently in tripped status at the same tick. Members can trip, un-trip (geofence "both" trigger re-exit; manual dismiss), and re-trip independently during the flight without breaking the group's progress — the check is just "are they all currently tripped right now?". While the group is incomplete, individual trips are silent (no overlay, no sound, no mobile push) but show a small ✓ pip on the card so you can see progress. Best for "all these conditions must currently be true at the same moment" scenarios — e.g. [🧩 Conditional: GS > 400] + [🧩 Conditional: alt > FL350] fires only while both are currently true. Dismissal behaviour (v2.95.0+): dismissing a fired CONCURRENT group re-arms all members (clears tripped status, resets transient state) so each member can independently re-trip later in the flight if its condition fires again. The group itself does NOT re-fire this mission — the per-group _fired flag stays set until the next ground transition. So you get one composite alert per group per flight, but the underlying alarms remain useful as individual sentries for the rest of the mission.
  • SEQUENTIAL (new in v2.92.0) — each member's first trip is silently noted and persists for the rest of the flight (via the per-alarm state.everTripped flag), even if that member is later dismissed individually or its condition reverts. The ✓ pip on the card stays lit. When every enabled member has tripped at any point this mission (sequentially is fine — no overlap required), the group fires its single composite alert. Best for compound-milestone scenarios where the events happen at different times — e.g. [Cruise Reached] + [Waypoint TOD] + [Altitude descending through FL100] fires only after all three have happened during the flight. Ground transition clears the ✓ pip flags so the group re-arms for the next flight. Same v2.95.0 dismissal behaviour as CONCURRENT: dismissing a fired sequential group re-arms members; group doesn't re-fire this mission.
Dismissal — mode-aware (v2.95.0+): CASCADE mode dismisses disable every member (the documented "one fires, rest go silent" contract). CONCURRENT / SEQUENTIAL mode dismisses re-arm every member (members can keep tripping individually for the rest of the flight; the group's _fired flag prevents the composite alert from re-firing until ground transition). A mode-aware toast surfaces the impact: CASCADE shows "dismissed N siblings (X also-tripped + Y still-armed)"; non-cascade shows "Group fired. N members re-armed — group will not re-fire this mission." Save / Load JSON files preserve the mode; the legacy and value from v2.91.0 reads back as concurrent.

Hover a collapsed card to see a tooltip summary of its current settings (sound, duration, alarm-specific params) without expanding it.

Collapse cards to declutter — each card has a small chevron button in its top-right action bar that toggles the editor body (sound picker, duration, type-specific inputs). The card header (icon, title, subtitle) stays visible so you can monitor every alarm at a glance. Collapsed state is per-card and survives reloads. Newly-added cards default to expanded so the configuration options are immediately visible. Cards imported from file default to collapsed so a large saved set doesn’t flood the modal; expand any card individually to review or adjust its settings.

When an alarm trips a full-screen overlay appears with a pulsing red ALARM header listing every alarm currently tripped. The sound loops continuously until you click DISMISS ALL. The browser tab title also flashes ⚠ ALARM — SimFly as a cross-window cue. Only one alarm sound plays at a time — the highest-priority alarm wins, so additional alarms that pile on just get added to the overlay list.

Enable All / Disable All (modal footer, next to Delete All) flip every configured alert in one click. Disable All disarms everything, removes geofence circles and waypoint highlights from the map, and stops any active sound — configuration is preserved so a follow-up Enable All (v2.88.0+) re-arms the whole queue and reschedules any timer countdowns. Delete All permanently removes every alert from the queue. The per-card ✕ button does the same for a single alert. The footer has two rows: row 1 holds the tool buttons (Test Sound, Alert History, Group selected) | Load Alerts / Save Alerts | Load Defaults / Save Defaults; row 2 holds the three bulk state-flip buttons (Enable All, Disable All, Delete All).

Load Alerts behaviour (v2.87.1+) — importing an alarm file always produces a ready-to-fire configuration: every imported alarm is set to enabled regardless of whatever enabled state was saved in the file, and per-type transient state (cruise / descent / alt-min fired sentinels, geofence wasInside, waypoint trippedFixes) is freshly seeded so the next live tick treats each alarm as just-armed. Airborne import warning: if the aircraft is actively flying (live source connected and groundspeed > 30 kt, or |VS| > 100 fpm, or wheels-up confirmed) AA shows a confirmation dialog before applying the import — some alarm types can fire on the first tick post-load if the current flight state already satisfies their conditions (e.g., a Cruise alarm with a target altitude below your current FL). You can Cancel or Load Anyway.

⭐ Default Alerts (v3.33.0) — configure your go-to alert set once, then have it load automatically with every flight plan. Three steps: (1) build your standard alerts in the Alerts modal; (2) click ⭐ Save Defaults in the footer to save them to localStorage (it asks before overwriting an existing default set, and is disabled when no alerts are configured); (3) enable ⭐ Auto-load Default Alerts in the 🔌 Live menu. From that point on, loading a SimBrief flight plan replaces the queue with your defaults automatically. The same auto-load fires at app startup if the queue is empty. You can also pull your saved defaults into the modal at any time with ⭐ Load Defaults (disabled when none are saved; it replaces the current queue). Safeguards: auto-load is silently skipped if the aircraft is airborne (groundspeed > 30 kt, vertical speed > 100 fpm, or wheels-up confirmed) — your in-mission alerts are never touched. Re-fetching the same plan (same origin, destination, and waypoint count) after you have already customised your alerts also skips the re-apply, so adding alarms on top of the defaults is safe. The chooser screen’s Load SimBrief Flight Plan card shows the same checkbox when defaults are saved, giving you a one-click way to toggle the setting right before you load. Right-click ⭐ Load Defaults to clear saved defaults and reset the toggle. Defaults include your Mobile Alerts provider configuration so the whole setup travels with a single save.

Persistence — alarms are saved to localStorage and survive page reloads (with their enabled state preserved — that's distinct from file import, which always re-enables everything). If the loaded SimBrief flight plan changes (different origin/destination/fix count), any Specific waypoints picks are automatically cleared (since the old fix idents no longer apply) and you’re notified by toast.

🔊 Test Sound (v2.87.0, picker in v2.88.0) — a button at the bottom of the Alerts modal that previews one of the five built-in sounds for ~2.5 seconds so you can verify audio is working without arming an alarm. Plain click cycles through the library. Shift-click or right-click opens a popup picker so you can preview any specific sound directly. If an alarm is actively tripped and sounding when you click, the preview takes over briefly and the original alarm sound resumes automatically afterwards.

🔊→All — bulk set sound or duration (duration added v3.3.0) — a button at the bottom of the Alerts modal opens a two-section picker. The top section applies one chosen sound to every alarm at once; the bottom section sets the Sound for auto-dismiss duration (Indefinite, or 5 / 10 / 30 / 60 seconds) on every alarm at once. A toast confirms how many alarms changed.

📜 Alert History (v2.87.0) — a separate modal (also opened from the bottom of the Alerts modal) showing the last 100 alert events on this device. Each row captures the local + UTC timestamp the alarm tripped, the alarm type and label, every configurable parameter that was set on the alarm, the eventual dismissal reason (user dismissed, duration timer, or group cascade), and a full aircraft telemetry snapshot at trip time (position, altitude, GS, IAS, heading, vertical speed, phase, source, aircraft ident). Per-row 📍 Pin to Map drops a labelled gold pin at the captured lat/lon so you can review where each alert fired during the flight; click again to remove the pin. Per-row ✕ deletes that single entry. ⤓ Save Log (v3.17.0+) in the footer saves all entries to a JSON file on disk regardless of the current filter — useful for post-flight review or sharing with someone for troubleshooting. Clear History wipes the whole log. The log is FIFO — once full, the oldest entry is evicted to make room for each new event. Storage is local to this browser (localStorage key simfly_aa_afk_alert_history); it does not sync across machines and survives reloads. Group cascades are recorded fully: when one alarm in a group fires, its own entry is logged with role group fired; each sibling that was also tripped is marked dismissed via group cascade; and each armed sibling that was auto-disabled by the cascade gets a synthetic entry noting it never actually fired but was auto-dismissed because a different alarm in the group did.

High-elevation airport guards (v2.87.0) — ground detection now uses GS + VS instead of the old MSL altitude < 1000 ft test, so taxi at airports like MMAS (6,112 ft) or El Alto (13,300 ft) no longer fools the phase detector into thinking the aircraft is airborne. As a backstop, Cruise Reached and Descent Started additionally require a confirmed wheels-up event (sustained GS > 40 kt for 10 s) before either can fire.

Sources required — Alerts need a live aircraft source (FSUIPC Live or STKP Live) to evaluate. Waypoint, TOC, and TOD alarms additionally need a SimBrief flight plan loaded.
📱
Mobile Alerts
Send AA alarm trips straight to your phone so you can step away from the sim and still know when something fires. Open 🔌 Live → 📱 Mobile Alerts… in the map header, pick a provider, fill in the fields, click ▶ Test to confirm delivery, then Save. From that point on, every alarm card in 🔔 Alerts gains a Mobile alert toggle — flip it on for any alarm you want forwarded to your phone.

Provider presets (pick one):
  • ntfy.sh — free, no account. Install the ntfy app on your phone, subscribe to the topic name you choose, and you’re done. The topic IS the channel — anyone who guesses it can read your alerts, so pick something unguessable (the app suggests random strings; the input is now capped at 8 characters). Priority defaults to Max (sound + bypass Do Not Disturb) so alerts wake you. The triggering alarm type is appended to the notification title automatically (e.g. “SimFly Active Airports - Top of Descent”). Title characters are Latin-1 only — ntfy carries the title in an HTTP header so anything outside that range (CJK, emoji, etc.) is stripped as you type. Smart-quote, em-dash and ellipsis are auto-normalized to ASCII so you don’t lose meaning. Best for getting going in under a minute with zero setup cost. Every other provider in the dropdown is marked (beta).
  • Pushover — one-time US$5 per platform (iOS / Android / desktop). Install Pushover, copy your User Key, create an application at pushover.net/apps for an API Token, paste both in. Most polished delivery of the bunch — per-message priorities, custom alert sounds, and emergency re-alerting until acknowledged.
  • Discord webhook — in any Discord channel: Edit Channel → Integrations → Webhooks → New Webhook → Copy URL. Paste the URL. Alerts land as messages in that channel, and Discord’s mobile app handles the push notification — enable channel notifications on your phone. Optionally override the webhook’s display name with a Username field.
  • Telegram bot — chat with @BotFather in Telegram and run /newbot to get a Bot Token. Then message your bot once and visit api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/getUpdates in a browser to find your numeric Chat ID. Telegram bots can only send to chats they’ve been added to.
  • Slack webhook — create an Incoming Webhook at api.slack.com/apps for your workspace and paste the resulting URL. Alerts land in the configured channel; the Slack mobile app pushes them as long as you have channel notifications enabled.
  • Custom webhook — any service that accepts an HTTP POST. Pick a Content-Type (application/json, x-www-form-urlencoded, or text/plain) and write a body template using {{message}} as the placeholder for the alert text. Covers self-hosted ntfy, Gotify, Home Assistant webhooks, IFTTT webhooks, and anything else. The receiving service must allow browser-origin POSTs (CORS).
Test before you trust it. The modal’s ▶ Test button sends a real notification using the values currently in the form (without saving them), and the status line below confirms "✓ Test sent. Check your phone." on success or shows the HTTP error on failure. Run this before relying on the setup — it’s much better to discover a wrong topic / token / URL while still in the dialog than during a 7-hour cruise.

Per-alarm opt-in. The Mobile alert toggle on each alarm card is independent — you might want Descent Started and Geofence (Arrival) forwarded to your phone but skip a frequent Timer alarm. Every newly-tripped alarm with the toggle on fires its own notification, so you get one buzz per event — even within a group. v3.3.0: the Geofence and Add Waypoint Alarm quick-add pop-ups now include a Send to phone checkbox so you can opt these in at creation time.

Menu badge. Once a provider is configured, the Live → 📱 Mobile Alerts menu row shows a red count badge of how many active (enabled) alarms are set to forward to your phone — an at-a-glance confirmation that something will actually reach you while you’re away. It hides when the count is zero or no provider is configured.

Mobile Alerts adds to, never replaces. The in-tab alarm sound, full-screen overlay, and tab-title flash all behave exactly as before. The phone push is fire-and-forget on top — if the POST fails (offline, bad token, CORS), AA toasts a soft warning naming the alarm and the error, but the alarm itself still trips normally.

Storage and privacy. Provider settings are stored locally in localStorage under the key aa_mobile_alerts_v1. Tokens, webhook URLs, and topic names never leave your browser except as the body of the HTTPS POST to the provider you configured. Click Clear in the modal to wipe the settings entirely — the per-alarm Mobile alert toggles also disappear from every alarm card, since the row only renders when a provider is configured.

Travel with your alarms. The 🔔 Alerts modal's ⤓ Save Alerts button bundles your Mobile Alerts provider configuration alongside the alarm queue in the exported JSON, and ⤒ Load Alerts applies it on the way back in. The ⭐ Save Defaults button saves the same bundle to localStorage for auto-load on plan load (and ⭐ Load Defaults pulls it back into the modal on demand). One file is enough to recreate the whole setup on another machine. If the file's Mobile Alerts settings would overwrite a different existing configuration on load, AA asks first — you can choose to keep your current settings. Older save files that predate v2.85.0 still import cleanly and leave the current Mobile Alerts config alone. Note that exported files contain whatever tokens / webhook URLs / topic names you have configured, so they’re fine for personal backup but worth being careful with when shared.
〰️
Aircraft Track / Trail
While a live aircraft source (FSUIPC or STKP) is active, AA draws a green breadcrumb trail behind the aircraft showing the actual path you've flown. The trail is a two-layer polyline (outer glow + bright inner core) that stays visible on both street and satellite map backgrounds, and is intentionally a different colour from the SimBrief route line so deviations from the plan are obvious at a glance.

Two-tier storage — the trail uses a smart sampling strategy so it can record any real-world flight without burning memory:
  • Fresh tier — last ~75 nm at fine detail (0.05 nm spacing, ~300 ft between points). This is what you see when you zoom in to inspect a recent turn, hold, or pattern.
  • Archive tier — older history at coarser detail (1 nm spacing). As points age out of the fresh tier they're either added to the archive or dropped if they're within 1 nm of the previous archive point. Straight cruise legs collapse from thousands of fresh samples to far fewer archive points without losing the visible shape of the trail.
Total capacity: 11,500 points (~184 KB memory) covering ~10,000 nm — enough for any non-stop commercial flight on Earth, including KJFK→YSSY (~9,950 nm) and SIN→EWR (~9,500 nm). The trail auto-clears when the live source is stopped, and resets if a large position jump is detected (sim restart or loading a new flight).
📊
Aircraft Data Panel
A persistent strip at the bottom of the map that streams live flight parameters in real time. Three sections separated by vertical dividers:

Position & motion — LAT, LON, HDG, ALT MSL, GS, IAS, VS, PHASE, and NEXT (the ident of the next un-passed waypoint on the loaded SimBrief route; v3.6.0 — shows — without a plan). A TRK DEV field (v3.6.2 — cross-track distance in nm abeam the SimBrief route) is also available but hidden by default; add it from the ⚙ menu under Navigation. ALT MSL is the true altitude above mean sea level (FSUIPC offset 0x0574). IND ALT was attempted in v2.79.1 / v2.80.1 and removed in v2.80.2 — FSUIPC offset 0x6020 didn't return usable values through Paul Henty's WebSocket on real-world setups (both type:'double' and bytearray decode left the field at —); a SimVar-passthrough approach is the likely path forward if this is ever revisited. AGL was removed in v2.76.1 because the FSUIPC offset it relied on (0x0B4C) frequently returns the nearest-airport reference elevation rather than the actual terrain under the aircraft — readings were unreliable over hills, lakes, or anywhere away from a runway. Vertical speed is computed locally from a rolling altitude buffer (so it works the same for FSUIPC and STKP) and shown with a directional arrow + magnitude. PHASE is sourced from STKP when present, otherwise computed locally by the same adaptive detector used by the alarms.

Flight progress — ELAPSED (clock time since takeoff — detected when GS first exceeds 40 kt sustained for 10 s; resets on landing or when a new SimBrief flight plan is loaded on the ground). As of v2.78.0 the wheels-up timestamp persists to localStorage so ELAPSED survives browser reloads mid-flight — the new-flight detector clears it cleanly when you load a fresh SimBrief plan with the aircraft on the ground. DIST TO DEST (great-circle nm directly to the destination — straight line, ignores any planned dogleg) and TRK MILES REM (track miles remaining along the SimBrief route — current position to the next un-passed fix + every remaining leg through the destination). REMAINING and ETA LOCAL / ETA UTC are computed from track miles, so a route that doglegs around a TMA reports the time you'll actually fly rather than the straight-line under-read. Falls back to the great-circle figure when the plan has no navlog fixes. Requires a SimBrief flight plan for the destination-relative fields; ELAPSED works without one. In Flight Demo Mode (v3.6.5) ELAPSED shows simulated time since takeoff — matching the scrub bar — rather than wall-clock, so a sped-up demo reports the flight time it represents.

Next alarm — shows which armed alarm will trip soonest and how long until it does (H:MM:SS), computed each tick from current position and ground speed. Geofence, waypoint, TOC, TOD, and Altitude alarms are ETA-predictable; the Cruise Reached alarm is predictable when you've set a target cruise altitude (climb-through ETA from current alt + VS); the Descent Started alarm is predictable when a SimBrief plan is loaded and you're well above the destination (rough-guess ETA-to-TOD using the classic "3× rule": descent distance in nm ≈ altitude-to-lose in thousands of feet × 3, with a 1,500 ft pattern buffer — works across light singles through heavy jets without per-aircraft tuning). Phase Change alarms remain non-predictable. The countdown pulses red when under 60 seconds. Geofence and waypoint next-alarm names are clickable — click the name (dotted underline) to smooth-fly the map to that alarm's location; the click also turns Follow A/C off so the recentre actually sticks.

The panel starts collapsed on every page load and auto-expands as soon as live data begins flowing (FSUIPC connected or demo started); it returns to collapsed when the source stops. Click the small ⏷ chevron to toggle it manually at any time. The panel renders in Rajdhani with tabular-numeric digits, and every value column has a per-field min-width sized to its worst-case readout, so the layout stays rock-steady even as units, signs, and digit counts change.

⚙ Customise the panel (v2.80.0+) — click the gear icon next to the ⏷ chevron to open a small popover with checkboxes for every field, grouped by category (Alerts, Altitude, Distance, Navigation, Position, Speed, Status, Time) and alphabetised within each group. Tick or untick any box to show/hide that field on the bar. The popover also has + Add Spacer to drop another vertical-bar divider in (you can add as many as you want), and Reset to Default to restore the original layout. Drag to reorder — hover any field and a small ⋮⋮ grip fades in above it; grab it and drag left/right to move the field anywhere. Spacers are draggable too. Your layout persists to localStorage and is restored every time the map loads.

FSUIPC offset note — IAS reads FSUIPC offset 0x02BC (uint, knots × 128). STKP doesn’t expose IAS separately, so the IAS field shows — when STKP is the active source.
💾
Save & Load All Settings (Master Save File)
Back up everything Active Airports remembers on this device into a single file, and restore it whenever you like (v3.65.0+). It captures your Scenery Library, Alerts, aircraft and flight-time calibration, color palettes, map filters and overlays, SimBrief and FSUIPC preferences, alert defaults, and the rest — useful for moving to a new browser or computer, or just keeping a safety copy. Open it from the ⚙ Settings button at the top of the Flight Planner, or from 🛠 Tools → Save All Settings / Load All Settings on the map.

Saving — a dialog lists your settings grouped into tidy categories, each with a checkbox; expand any group with its ▶ triangle to tick individual settings. Scenery Library and Alerts appear at the top as their own optional toggles, so you choose per-backup whether to bundle that feature’s data. Use Select All / Clear All to flip everything at once. A confirmation screen summarises what will be written before the file is saved to disk.

Loading — pick a master save file and the same grouped, expandable checklist appears (everything ticked by default), showing only what the file actually contains. The confirmation screen is a before-and-after table: every setting to be restored is shown with its current value next to the incoming one, flagged NEW, CHANGED, or same (with a “hide unchanged” toggle). Nothing is altered until you confirm, and any setting not present in the file is left untouched. After restoring, the app reloads so every change takes effect. Every screen in both flows has a Cancel button.

Version-proof — the file stores settings in a version-agnostic way, so a save written by a newer build of the app can be loaded by an older one and vice-versa, and any version can open any master save file whether or not it includes Scenery Library or Alerts data.
💾  Settings

Save every setting on this device to a single master save file, or restore everything from one you saved earlier.

Settings
📋  Release Notes
Show:
No releases match the selected filters.
v3.69.1 June 29, 2026 · Current Improved
  • Toast notifications now have an ✕ close button. The little pop-up messages (Recip Tracker saves, FSUIPC status, live-flight logging, Scenery Library actions, and the rest) used to only fade out on their own — and could occasionally linger on screen if the browser throttled the timer while AA was in the background. Each toast now carries an ✕ you can click to dismiss it instantly.
  • Lingering toasts can no longer block clicks. The close button is only active while the toast is actually visible, so a faded-out toast never sits invisibly on top of the buttons behind it.
v3.69.0 June 29, 2026 Feature
  • New “Lock Distance” option for measurements. The floating Measurement toolbar now has a 🔓 Lock Distance toggle. When you drag a measurement to a new spot, both its reported distance and its on-screen heading could previously shift — that’s map scale at work, and it got dramatic for long lines (the line could visibly swing by tens of degrees as you moved it toward higher latitudes). Turn Lock Distance on and a relocated measurement keeps a constant true heading and its measured distance: the line slides without rotating and the nm/km and bearing labels stay put.
  • How it works. A locked measurement moves as a constant-heading line (a rhumb line) — the same straight line a navigator plots on a chart. Because a constant-heading line draws straight on the map, it never rotates no matter where you move it, at any latitude. The distance shown is held to within a fraction of a percent of your original measurement.
  • Off by default, and it sticks. Leave it off for the classic behaviour (the measurement drags rigidly and its distance and heading follow the map scale). Flip it on and the choice is remembered across reloads and rides along in your Master Save File. It applies to standalone measurements; conjoined route legs still pivot around their shared waypoints as before, and resizing a locked measurement by its edge handle returns it to normal great-circle behaviour.
v3.68.0 June 29, 2026 Feature
  • File on SimBrief is now a live “stop-sign” button. Both File-on-SimBrief buttons (the main planner button and the floating map route pill) no longer sit a fixed orange. They fill with a high-contrast color (white text on green/red/blue, dark text on amber) — a polished gradient with a glossy top edge and a soft state-coloured glow — reflecting the health of your current departure → arrival route for the selected aircraft, so you can read the go/no-go at a glance before filing.
  • Green — good to go. The aircraft clears suitability at both ends (runway length, range, category), the weather is VFR, rotations are available, and the aircraft isn’t stranded at a different airport in the owner’s fleet.
  • Blue (glowing) — your aircraft is waiting at departure. Everything green, and the selected aircraft is rentable right now at the departure airport. The button pulses and its hover tooltip tells you the aircraft is available there.
  • Amber — caution. Something is worth a second look: 3 or fewer rotations left at an endpoint, non-VFR (MVFR/IFR) weather, the aircraft isn’t at your departure but is at your arrival (it suggests swapping the two), or the aircraft isn’t at your departure but the departure or arrival owner has it at a third airport (other than this route’s departure or arrival).
  • Red — not recommended. A hard stop: the aircraft is unsuitable for the departure or arrival (runway/range/category), or an endpoint has zero weekly rotations left.
  • Cautions vs Notes in the tooltip. An amber button’s hover now separates genuine Cautions (weather, wind, low rotations, thin range) from informational Notes (your aircraft is available at the arrival or elsewhere in the owner’s fleet) — so an opportunity never reads as a warning.
  • Hover for the reason. The button’s tooltip spells out exactly what tripped the color — the specific failed check, not just “unsuitable.” You’ll see the actual shortfall (e.g. route 540 nm exceeds C172 range 150 nm, longest runway 3,200 ft < ~4,500 ft needed), the weather’s limiting factor (IFR — ceiling 600 ft, vis 2 SM), low rotations, or the aircraft-elsewhere situation — or the all-clear when green.
  • More conditions feed the color. Amber now also catches strong or gusting surface wind (sustained ≥25 kt or gusts ≥30 kt — you can be VFR yet have an unflyable gust) and a thin range margin (the route uses 85–100% of the aircraft’s range, i.e. little reserve). When an airport has no runway data the tooltip adds a neutral “suitability unverified” note so green is never falsely confident.
  • Same-airport routes are disabled, not coloured. If departure and arrival are the same airport the button greys out (it can’t file a zero-distance plan), exactly like an incomplete route — keeping red reserved for genuine aircraft/airport problems.
v3.67.4 June 28, 2026 Improved
  • Aircraft Listing opens compact and right-sized. The panel now opens just wide enough to fit every column heading — and no wider — so there’s no truncated headings and no wasted, empty window space (a big improvement on wide / ultrawide monitors). Longer cell values (full aircraft type names, owner ids) are trimmed with “…”; hover a cell to read the full text, or drag a column wider.
  • Auto-fits until you take over. Every open re-fits the columns and panel width to the headings. Once you drag a column or resize the panel, your sizing sticks; double-click a column divider any time to snap everything back to the compact title fit.
v3.67.3 June 28, 2026 Fix
  • Edge-badge hover card no longer slides under the badge. Hovering an aircraft-availability badge reveals its 🛫 / 🛬 set-departure / set-arrival icons, which widens the badge by about 56 px. The card’s position was based on the badge’s resting (narrower) width, so once it grew on hover it reached back under the card. The card is now placed using the badge’s full hovered width, so it stays clear on every edge.
v3.67.2 June 28, 2026 Improved
  • Aircraft Listing now separates Airport Owner from Aircraft Owner. The old “Owner” column is now Airport Owner (the SimFly pilot who owns the airport), and a new Aircraft Owner column (the pilot who owns the aircraft) sits to the right of Type. They’re often different — an aircraft can be parked at someone else’s airport. Both sort and multi-filter like every other column.
  • Clearer owner tooltips. Clicking an Airport Owner reads “Click to see aircraft at [owner]’s airports,” and clicking an Aircraft Owner reads “Click to see [owner]’s aircraft” — each noting that a second click removes the filter.
  • Edge badges no longer covered by their hover card. For aircraft-availability badges pinned to the left or right screen edge, the info card is now sized to the exact badge width so it always opens clear of it — the wider badges (with payout and a four-letter ICAO) are no longer obscured.
v3.67.1 June 28, 2026 Fix
  • Edge badges no longer hidden by their own pop-up. For aircraft-availability badges pinned to the screen edge, the hover info card opened right on top of the badge (and a small “Click to pan” box covered it too). The card now opens clear of the badge, and that extra box is gone — the badge stays fully readable.
  • Off-screen departure/arrival badge now shows reliably. When a selected departure or arrival was panned off-screen, its edge label could land directly under another badge and disappear. All edge badges are now spread apart together so they never cover each other, and the departure/arrival label appears whenever there’s a line leading back to it.
v3.67.0 June 28, 2026 Improved
  • Aircraft-availability badges now show the payout %. When you select an aircraft and a departure/arrival airport, the green badges over the owner’s other airports that have that aircraft rentable now also display the highest available pilot payout % there (color-coded on the rental spectrum). At rest the badge reads aircraft · airport · payout%; on hover the 🛫 / 🛬 set-as-departure / set-as-arrival icons appear on either side.
  • Off-screen departure/arrival now labels its own line. When you pan to one of those other airports, the selected departure or arrival often scrolls off-screen, leaving its dashed connector running to the edge with no label. There’s now a badge on that edge — cyan for the departure, orange for the arrival — showing which airport the line leads back to. Click it to pan straight back.
v3.66.0 June 28, 2026 Feature
  • New “Aircraft Listing” in the 🛩 Rentals menu. Opens a big, resizable, draggable panel (about 60% of the map) with a full table of every rentable aircraft — one row each, showing its airport, owner, aircraft ICAO, manufacturer, type, pilot payout %, and availability. For aircraft in ground operations the Available column shows a live hours / minutes / seconds countdown until it frees up, and the Avail Time (Local) column shows the local clock time it becomes available.
  • Sort and filter any column. Click a column heading to sort A–Z, click again for Z–A. Each heading’s ▾ opens a multi-select filter; the Payout % heading sets a minimum payout, the Airport heading sets a minimum airport category, and Available filters by Yes / No.
  • The table drives the map. Whenever you filter the table, the map updates to show only the airport dots still in the table. Click an airport to pan to it; click an owner, aircraft ICAO, manufacturer, or type to instantly narrow to just those — click the same value again to clear it.
  • Adjustable columns and opacity. Drag any column divider to resize it, or double-click a divider to auto-fit every column to the wider of its heading or its widest value. A slider in the title bar fades the whole panel between 10% and 100% opacity so you can see the map underneath — the Aircraft Rentals panel gains the same opacity slider. A ↺ Reset button clears all filters and shows every aircraft again. Your sort, column widths, filters, opacity, and panel size are remembered between sessions.
v3.65.1 June 28, 2026 Fix
  • Save / Load All Settings dialogs no longer open behind the map. When launched from the map’s 🛠 Tools menu, the new settings dialogs were rendering underneath the interactive map instead of on top of it. They now sit above the map like every other map-opened panel.
v3.65.0 June 28, 2026 Feature
  • New “Save All Settings” / “Load All Settings” master save file. Back up everything the app remembers on this device — your Scenery Library, Alerts, aircraft calibration, palettes, filters, SimBrief and FSUIPC preferences, and the rest — into a single file on disk, then restore it any time. Reach it from the new ⚙ Settings button on the main planner, or from 🛠 Tools → Save / Load All Settings on the map.
  • Pick exactly what to include. Both the save and load screens group settings into tidy categories with a checkbox each; expand any group to tick individual settings. Scenery Library and Alerts sit at the top as their own optional toggles, so you decide per-backup whether to bundle them. Select All / Clear All are one click, and every screen has a Cancel.
  • See what a restore will overwrite before it happens. The load confirmation lists each setting side-by-side — your current value next to the incoming one — flagged NEW, CHANGED, or same, with a “hide unchanged” toggle. Nothing is touched until you confirm, and settings not in the file are left exactly as they are.
  • Works across app versions, both directions. The file stores settings in a version-agnostic way, so a master save written by a newer build loads in an older one (and vice-versa), and any version can open a file whether or not it carries Scenery Library or Alerts data.
v3.64.0 June 28, 2026 Improved
  • Scenery Library search now covers Store, Developer, and Notes. The search box at the top of the Scenery Library used to match only ICAO, airport name, and add-on title. It now also searches the Store, Developer, and Notes fields — so you can type a developer like “Orbx”, a store, or any word you jotted in a note and instantly filter the list to matching airports.
v3.63.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • New “Improve accuracy with live flight data” option for flight-time estimates. Each aircraft’s Flight Time Calibration panel now has an opt-in checkbox. When it’s on, real flights you fly with FSUIPC connected are captured automatically — the airborne time (wheels-up to wheels-down, taxi excluded) and the actual distance flown — and blended with your two reference flights to sharpen the en-route time estimates shown across the app. It’s per-aircraft and off by default; nothing changes unless you turn it on.
  • Captures only the airborne leg, and only clean data. A new telemetry reading (the sim’s on-ground flag) marks exactly when you leave and touch the ground, so taxi never pollutes the numbers. If telemetry drops (for example when you open the in-sim menu) the timer and distance both pause; if it’s gone for more than five minutes the flight is discarded rather than logged with a gap.
  • Gets smarter the more you fly — without one distance drowning out the others. Flights are grouped into ~100 nm distance bands, each keeping your most recent flights, and the estimate is fitted across all your bands plus your reference flights. So ten flights at the same distance sharpen that point instead of skewing the whole line, and your spread of short and long flights is always preserved.
  • “View data” button — see every captured flight and the resulting calculation. Opens a panel with a graph of your flights (and reference points) with the calculated line drawn through them, plus a dated table of each flight’s distance and time. Remove any individual flight, or reset an aircraft’s live data entirely.
  • Live flights are matched to the right aircraft automatically. A finished flight is credited to your loaded SimBrief plan’s aircraft type when there is one, otherwise to the aircraft selected in the panel.
v3.62.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • New “Only airports already on the map” toggle in the Aircraft Rentals panel. When on, the rentals feature considers only SimFly-owned airports already shown on the map (still subject to any filters you have active) instead of pulling in every rental-only airport from across the world — so the map dots, the results table, the type/owner counts, and the plane/airport totals all stay limited to your airports. Off by default; your choice is remembered.
v3.61.0 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • The 🛫 icon on a rental badge now slides the old departure into the arrival slot. When you tap the little departure-plane (🛫) icon on an aircraft-rental badge while a departure is already selected, the previous departure automatically becomes the arrival — so you can build a route just by tapping 🛫 on one badge after another (the airport you just picked becomes the departure, the one before it becomes the arrival). This is scoped to the badge’s 🛫 icon only; setting a departure any other way (matrix row header, radial menu, map-dot click, typed ICAO) is unchanged, as are all arrival clicks.
v3.60.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • Airport & rental data now stays fresh while the app is open. Until now, owner colors, levels, rotations, for-sale flags, and especially rentable-aircraft availability were loaded once when the app started and never refreshed — so a tab left open for hours showed a stale snapshot (a plane rented out by someone else would still appear available). The app now quietly re-fetches these feeds in the background every 20 minutes, plus the moment you enter Aircraft Rentals mode and whenever you return to a tab that’s been idle. The refresh happens in place: no page reload, no flash, and your map position, selected airports, routes, regions, filters, and SimBrief plan are all preserved. It only repaints when the data has actually changed, and it politely waits if you’re mid-interaction (typing, an open popover) so it never interrupts you.
  • Freshness indicator in the Rentals panel. A small “↻ Rental data synced N min ago” line now sits at the bottom of the Aircraft Rentals panel, just under the plane/airport totals, so you always know how current the view is. It turns amber if syncing falls behind, and you can click it to refresh on demand.
v3.59.2 June 27, 2026 Fix
  • Alarm sounds no longer lag in a background tab. When the app sat in a background browser tab (common while you’re looking at the sim), Edge/Chrome throttle page timers — eventually to just once a minute — which made repeating AFK alarm tones stutter with long, irregular gaps. Alarm audio is now scheduled ahead directly on the browser’s audio clock, so each repeat plays on time no matter how aggressively the tab is throttled. Dismissing, disabling, or deleting an alarm silences it instantly — the scheduled-ahead sound is cancelled the moment the alarm is turned off, so a cancelled alarm can never keep ringing.
v3.59.1 June 27, 2026 Fix
  • Off-screen rental badges no longer overlap. When several rentable airports sat off-screen near the same point, their edge badges could stack on top of each other. They’re now spread apart along the screen edge so each stays readable, with its connector line drawn to its final position.
v3.59.0 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • Flight Time Calibration is now a prominent card. Because this calibration feeds the en-route time estimates shown throughout the app (route lines, the map, measurements, and airport cards), it’s been promoted from a small footnote to a clearly highlighted ⏱ card with a short caption explaining what it powers. When the selected aircraft hasn’t been calibrated yet, the card turns amber and says so — an easy nudge to fill it in; once calibrated, it’s cyan with your live estimate.
v3.58.1 June 27, 2026 Fix
  • “Select Aircraft” focus now actually highlights the aircraft panel. The previous version’s dimming layer ended up covering the aircraft selector too, so nothing stood out. The focus now dims everything except the aircraft selector, which stays fully lit with a glowing outline — so your eye goes straight to it. Dismiss by picking an aircraft, clicking anywhere outside the panel, or pressing Esc.
  • Scenery Developers window no longer resizes while you filter. Typing in the developer filter box used to shrink or grow the window as the list got shorter or longer. The window is now a fixed size and the list scrolls inside it.
v3.58.0 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • “Select Aircraft” now spotlights the aircraft panel. When you use the Display menu’s Select Aircraft shortcut, the planner dims and the aircraft selector is lifted and highlighted — so you focus on picking an aircraft instead of being distracted by route or matrix information that may already be on screen (handy when a route is already selected). Nothing is changed underneath: the dim is purely visual and clears the moment you pick an aircraft, click anywhere outside the panel, or press Esc.
v3.57.2 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • Clearer guidance on the “Select Aircraft” menu item. Its tooltip now adds “Afterwards click the ⊙ Map button to return to this screen,” so it’s obvious how to get back to the map after picking an aircraft.
v3.57.1 June 27, 2026 Fix
  • Clearer wording for the rental availability line. When a pilot has no rentable copy of your aircraft, the airport tooltip now reads “none rentable from <pilot>’s airports now” instead of “none rentable from <pilot> now.” The check scans that pilot’s airports for the aircraft — it doesn’t require the aircraft to be owned by them — so the new wording reflects what’s actually being searched.
v3.57.0 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • Rental badges now show the standard airport tooltip. Hovering a rental badge now pops up the same compact tooltip a map dot shows, instead of the larger detailed airport card — which could run off the edge of the map and get clipped. Edge badges open their tooltip pointing inward so it always stays fully on screen.
  • “Select Aircraft” shortcut in the Display menu. Selecting an aircraft now unlocks meaningful functionality, so the Display-menu row is no longer greyed out when no aircraft is chosen. Instead it shows an active Select Aircraft item; its tooltip explains the aircraft drives time estimates, airport suitability, and alternate-airport recommendations. Clicking it closes the map and opens the aircraft selector panel so you can pick one. Once an aircraft is selected, the row becomes the usual Highlight other airports with <type> toggle.
v3.56.0 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • Arrival airport now shows rental lines and badges too. The rental highlight treated departure and arrival together, so when both belonged to the same owner all the dashed lines attached to whichever endpoint was closer — usually the departure — and the arrival appeared to have none. Departure and arrival are now handled independently: each draws its own dashed lines and badges to its owner’s airports where your aircraft is available, with full parity.
  • Off-screen badges keep working as you pan. Whenever a dashed line leads to an airport outside the current view, its edge badge now reliably appears at the screen border pointing toward that airport — including after you’ve panned the map far enough that the departure or arrival itself has scrolled off-screen.
v3.55.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • Rental badges now behave like airport dots — hover for the full info panel. Hovering a rental badge (on the map or on the screen edge) now pops up the same airport information panel the dots and radial menu show: name, owner, payout, runways, your aircraft’s suitability, and the rental availability line.
  • Set departure or arrival straight from a badge. Hovering a badge now reveals two small icons: a take-off plane (🛫) on the left sets that airport as your departure, and a landing plane (🛬) on the right sets it as your arrival — the same as picking it from the map. On touch devices the two icons are always shown (no hover needed). Off-screen edge badges keep their tap-to-pan behavior on the body.
v3.54.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • Connector lines to the airports where your aircraft is rentable. Building on the rental highlight from v3.53.0, the map now draws a faint dotted green line from your selected departure/arrival airport to each of that owner’s other airports where your aircraft is available to rent. The line is intentionally styled so it can’t be mistaken for a route, a measurement, or a distance line — it’s a thin, low-opacity dotted thread with no distance label.
  • Off-screen airports become one-tap “pan here” buttons. When a rentable airport is outside the current view, its line is drawn out to the edge of the map and the badge sits right there on the edge with an arrow pointing toward the airport. Click (or tap) it and the map pans to center that airport. The edge badges reposition automatically as you pan and zoom.
v3.53.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • See where your selected aircraft is rentable from an airport’s owner. Pick an aircraft in the main aircraft selector, then choose a departure or arrival airport on the map. Each airport’s tooltip now shows a green 🛩 <type> for rent line listing which of that owner’s airports have your aircraft available to rent right now — meaning no ground-operations timer is running — or a muted “none rentable from <owner> now” when there are none. It checks the owner’s whole fleet, not just the airport you’re hovering, and works in both the map view and the distance-matrix airport cards.
  • New map overlay — Highlight other airports with <type>. In the 👁 Display menu, a new overlay drops a badge directly on the other airports owned by your selected departure/arrival airport’s owner that have your aircraft rentable now — so you can see at a glance where to go pick it up. It appears once both an aircraft and a dep/arr airport are selected. The menu row shows the selected aircraft’s code (e.g. “Highlight other airports with C750”) and is grayed out with a hint until you select an aircraft.
v3.52.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • Filter the Scenery Developers list as you type. The ⚙ Developer Stats window now has a search box at the top — start typing a developer’s name and the list narrows live to matching developers, so you can find one in a long list without scrolling. Clearing the box restores the full list, and it resets each time you open the window.
  • Fix — the 🔍 developer drill now shows a clean list. Clicking the 🔍 next to a developer is meant to show that developer’s airports in the Scenery Library. Previously it kept whatever you’d last typed in the library’s search box and any active rated / unrated / owned mode filter, so the two combined and often hid the airports you expected. It now clears the search box and resets the mode filter first, so you land on exactly that developer’s airports every time.
  • Middle-click a radial-menu website spoke to open it in a background tab. The four website spokes (SimFly details, Sale page, flightsim.to, FS Addon Compare) already open in a background tab on right-click; now middle-click does the same. While the wheel is open the map is dimmed, so all clicks belong to the wheel — middle-click here intentionally takes priority over the map’s usual middle-click “show remaining rotations” gesture.
v3.51.1 June 27, 2026 Improved
  • The airport radial menu now shows the ICAO in its center. When you right-click an airport, the dot you clicked is labelled with its ICAO code right in the middle of the wheel, so there’s never any doubt which airport the menu’s actions apply to — handy in a crowded region where several dots sit close together.
  • Right-click a website spoke to open it in a background tab. The four spokes that open a website (SimFly details, Sale page, flightsim.to, FS Addon Compare) still open in a normal foreground tab when you click them, but now right-clicking one opens it in a background tab and leaves the wheel up — so you can fire off several at once (say flightsim.to and FS Addon Compare) and stay on the map, then dismiss the wheel when you’re done.
v3.51.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • New — right-click an airport for a radial menu. Right-clicking an airport dot (or long-pressing on touch) now opens a labelled wheel of everything you can do with that airport, instead of jumping straight to the scenery rating popover. The icons bloom outward from the dot, the rest of the map dims so the menu stays readable even in a crowded region, and the clicked airport keeps a bright ring so you always know which one you're acting on. Click any spoke to open it; click anywhere else, press Esc, or zoom to dismiss.
  • Seven actions, shown only when they apply. The wheel adapts to each airport: Airport info (a full stats / weather / aircraft-suitability card) and Scenery / rate always appear; SimFly details shows for owned airports, Airplanes (the rentable aircraft parked there) when there's rental data, and the Sale page when the airport is listed for sale. flightsim.to and FS Addon Compare round it out, each opening in a new tab. A plain airport shows four spokes; a fully-loaded one shows all seven.
  • Fast path for rating. If you rate a lot of scenery, you don't have to go through the wheel every time — double right-click an airport to jump straight into the rating popover, exactly as before.
  • Replaces the old for-sale shortcut. The previous “double right-click a for-sale airport to open its sale listing” gesture is retired; the sale page is now a labelled spoke on the wheel, and double right-click is the universal rating shortcut on every airport.
v3.50.1 June 27, 2026 Fix
  • Internal cleanup — Aircraft Rentals type lookup. Removed an obsolete code-to-code translation table that the Aircraft Rentals view no longer needs — the aircraft data file now uses the same type codes as the rentals feed, so types resolve directly. No change to what you see or do.
v3.50.0 June 27, 2026 Feature
  • New — Aircraft Rentals view. A new top-level 🛩 Aircraft Rentals mode (in the map menu) shows where SimFly's rentable aircraft are, refreshed automatically from a live feed. Turn it on and every airport's dot recolors by the best pilot payout available there (the cut the renting pilot keeps, 0–60%), and a Rentals panel opens for filtering and browsing. Closing the panel's ✕ leaves the rental info on the map; the menu item or the breadcrumb pill turns the whole view off.
  • On-map aircraft badges. Each airport can show a small stack of chips listing the aircraft types available there (best payout first, alphabetical). The map stays readable on its own: badges thin out when zoomed out and reveal more detail (up to ten chips per airport) as you zoom in, and when space is tight your own SimFly-owned airports get priority. Hover a badge for a full breakdown — type, registration, owner, payout and live availability for every matching aircraft.
  • Rich filtering panel.
